you a better programmer how true is this what is it about lisp that can make me much better at programming unlike learning a language like c c is actually another great example of a language that can make you a fundamentally better programmer so at the end of the day the downfall of some of the most common mid high level general purpose languages that everyone uses these days is they all kind of blend together after a little while languages like python javascript go java c ruby etc pick your poison spend so much time making sure they they re being nice and conservative and familiar once you learn one maybe two of those languages you basically know them all then you spend all your time bragging about language a has indentation instead of braces or interfaces instead of abstract classes or whatever stuff that doesn t really change the way you write code in any radical way maybe it adds more boilerplate or iboilerplate however when you learn c for the first time you learn a lot of things that you just would not encounter in any of those languages you get as close to learning how your computer actually works as you can without learning all about hardware you learn all about how memory is represented you probably pretty quickly learn about things like stack frames and heap allocation you learn how arrays and data structures work like actually work you learn a lot of things that other languages just kind of take for granted and it makes you much better at understanding what you re doing when you write code in any language lisps are the same way about different things lisps fundamentally challenge a lot of the assumptions that the big ball of generic popular languages make they ask much more fundamental questions than whether or not semicolons are a good thing languages have sort of borrowed half assed versions of many lisp ideas lambdas higher order functions immutable data recursion etc however you have to step to real functional programming languages which i m sort of conflating with lisps here to actually really understand this stuff the difference between lisps and other functional languages haskell ml etc while very present in e g macros is a lot smaller in comparison so honestly ml or haskell would teach you a lot as well but lisps are typically easy to learn so start here anyways then there are other languages that claim to be functional but either aren t javascript lol or are too conservative and cater to the imperative folks to their own detriment scala i m looking at you you will never full stop learn to write functional code by sticking with a conservative language even if you go back to javascript or python or whatever at the end of the day learning functional programming via python makes exactly as much sense as learning pointer arithmetic using python for instance i have never met someone who was comfortable using operations like reduce or writing recursion generally who hasn t done significant amounts of fp at some point there are a lot of people who can kind of sort of maybe cobble something together which vaguely works but it s the same as when you see someone just kind of frankensteining loop constructs together in the hope that they eventually arrive at a solution which seems to work based on testing gt what are the differences between all of the different dialects emacs lisp common lisp clojure etc so some of them have specific purposes for example emacs lisp is for writing emacs plugins assuming you don t have a specific use case that requires a specific implementation i recommend scheme if you want to treat this primarily as an educational opportunity and how to design programs as a text clojure if you want to build real programs even if you want to learn clojure working through scheme first to learn the core paradigms is probably a good idea you won t find it hard to transfer from scheme to clojure most of the complexity that clojure adds is due to stuff that it builds on top of scheme and isn t caused by wars over semicolons or curly braces gt what exactly is lisp best for i know from reading online that it was used primarily in ai research and was used to develop emacs but that was all i could find are there any other useful and practical applications that i can use lisp for clojure is the go to general purpose language in the family these days common lisp is also general purpose but afaik is all but dead and it really shows its age it s the c of lisps basically except that c still has a high user count common lisp is also not a functional language really so you won t learn as much you can do whatever you want with clojure it s good for a whole range of applications and has very easy java and javascript interop for pretty incredible flexibility i personally work in the language full time it s a lot of fun there aren t a ton of companies that use it but there are more than there are proficient programmers who are willing to work at those companies it s a good example of a niche which is hard to break into but comes with some demand once you do gt i ve heard that functional programming has a steep learning curve and can be a bit tricky to settle down in what are some tips you guys have for somebody who is more procedural and wants to be able to settle down in functional quickly work through htdp do all the exercises even the ones that seem like they re beneath you it starts from but if you skip ahead then before you know it you find the book asking you do recursion problems that people on this subreddit would complain are unfairly hard interview questions it will expect you to eventually be able to do those problems as fast as you can read them this is meant to be a first year textbook it isn t some sort of crazily advanced voodoo science it s just a very different way of thinking about code that most people never really explore']

Name: 0_data_data science_gt_gt gt
Representative_Docs: ['imo you re broaching the hot topic which is data science typically described as a mixture of computer science and statistics applied to business i say this because one can be a data analyst without any programming experience however to be a data scientist you are expected to be a competent programmer that being said the line between analyst and scientist is pretty fuzzy you can absolutely be a programmer data analyst i m going to use the term data science because that s the term that i am associated with i m in the second semester of my third year of my data science major i m also a data science intern at a small data science consulting company and the president of the data science club at my school i chose to pursue ds because it seemed more stimulating than software engineering data scientists are tasked with questions that they are expected to research independently instead of just being given issues that need to be resolved although this probably does happen to data scientists as well on occasion part of being a data scientist is spending time keeping yourself up to date researching the latest methods in machine learning etc i e a data scientist is always learning a data scientist gets to focus on a lot of the cool parts of programming algorithms machine learning prototyping without having to bother with a lot of the unattractive stuff unit testing etc that software engineers are focused on so that s why i went in this direction i don t have any complaints so far and i m really excited about the future if you were interested in taking this direction whether you think of yourself as an analyst scientist or whatever my advice would be to stay focus on learning programming and try to excel at it it s a skill that needs to be built over time this give you much more power than tableau provides even if you end up using tableau at work then i would get some basic familiarity in statistics and ml', 'you should work this problem in the opposite direction why do companies hire these people and what value would they get from them depending on the industry and even the team within a company these answers will be very different for example microsoft might have a data science team working with bing to optimize ad revenue and another team that works with customers to help them sell azure services as salespeople and product managers most data science teams earn their keep by cutting costs through automation ai increasing revenue through customer analysis or making the business more efficient among any other domain from employee metrics to maintenance to revenue forecasting it s basically treated as an extension of operations research that would have done things like optimize the routes of delivery trucks to save fuel etc most of the data science teams are started by some business person saying i heard you could save me some money and that s where the full stack data scientist would play this person effectively does it all to satisfy that goal but most importantly this person analyzes the business and formulates a hypothesis about how data driven approaches will accomplish this sometimes the data is complex enough that research into models techniques methods does happen but usually simple algorithms get them far enough that the marginal gain has to really be worth it at the end of the day this person does etl eda model building model execution at scale analysis and result presentation the final deliverable is a recommended action that the business takes that saves them money presented in a convincing form to business people possibly c level once this data science thing pans out into some real gains then this data scientist is given more resources to hire more people and expand the first thing this person will do is hire a data engineer to handle the programming aspects of this model building this person is handed a formulated model probably with a spreadsheet as an input and has to go write the sql queries to re build the spreadsheet transform the inputs to match the original model execute the model at scale distribute it parallelize improve performance and then provide the results or metrics on the results in another database and pass it back upstream there is some opportunity to collaborate with the scientist improve the models maybe but this person is not expected to understand the business or even know what the data means functionally to accomplish their tasks as this person matures then the skills will mainly grow software wise unless particular effort is taken to do the science part the data analyst in the traditional sense has been around much much longer than the data scientist and can be though of as an excel master or dashboard maker this person collects or maybe designs metrics about the business such as revenue per customer in a store and then sorts stores by that metric along with some other metrics for those stores like median income in the area and then presents these metrics to the business people usually without a recommendation just condensed data as data sources become more complex these people are asked to use sql and tools like tableau to make their reports and thus gain some skills in these areas but most things are simplified here toward a more business like person and less of an engineer scientist kind of role when it comes to education the true data scientist needs to exercise the scientific theory on business problems and so a phd is preferred it s not likely the degree is in data science instead the process of writing a dissertation is what is valued the data engineer needs a bs that gives enough software expertise to implement code on distributed systems although data pipelines have been simplified somewhat these people are really distributed systems people which is usually a ms level topic to master some knowledge of stats is okay but it just depends on how close this person is to a data scientist as defined above the data analyst is usually a bs business or ms springboard kind of position where other fields go get a data analysis ms from a business school kind of like an mba of sorts you d be expected to understand the particular business through some other background and then have the analysis skills from the ms ma program', 'hey there so i work in a data scientist position i will answer these questions from my point of view instead of a general point of view as an industry company growth and strategy and last but not the least job domain determine what a data scientist generally does and what are their backgrounds if you don t know yet data science in itself is a whole subject while it relies on computer science statistics numerical approximation on a standard basis different types of mathematics and domain knowledge i e the source and origin of data is also a very essential part of data science so i work in the machine learning part of data science on a regular basis i develop different sorts of machine learning models from data and using domain knowledge to solve very specific and defined business problems and all though i can t speak for all of them many data scientists i know do this same thing in different scenarios rather than a software work machine learning projects are much more unpredictable and therefore achievability of a machine learning project in one day month even in one year is not quite certain on a rather known statistics of all machine learning and data science projects end up in failure data scientists based on the variability of their works often use r python java spss julia and some other rare languages i work on python and r in combination python is well known easy to use rich in optimized libraries for machine learning and has remained as the popular choice for data scientists for the last couple of years after a good stint with r for obvious reasons i am not going to discuss my salary but the base salary for data scientists is pretty good in any market standard and can often be compared with medium to excellent level software engineers you can consider a k as average pay in the us for data scientists getting raise bonus and all sorts of things are very very company specific issues and therefore does not make sense to attach the concept of data scientists with that most of data scientists are from mathematical or engineering background i also come with a stat major with medium level expertise in python and different coding frameworks in short you should be familiar to read understand mathematical documents about different algorithms and should have enough programming knowledge to manipulate your data as well as use the standard algorithms already written by expert programmers while that may do most of the work data scientists often need to write their own algorithms and implement them in code so that should give you a good understanding of the pre requisites data science as interesting as it may sound maybe just a decade based phenomenon and most probably data science are going to become a standard skill for engineers and experts to have within years considering teenagers high schoolers being in a less than years age and more than years to stay in school still yes you definitely need to attend calculus classes in the college for being a good data scientist whatever young entrepreneurs may say it is pretty much as good as good old programming skills backend front end and full stack developer roles so as a career perspective for young people like high schoolers it is going to be not an extra ordinary but another choice in a tray of tough skills for high paying jobs but does that mean you don t need to learn it no data science all though the bubble may burst in incoming years just like the internet bubble did will become used more than ever in our world every business institute policy software and all the aspects of quantifiable lifestyle will use data science and ai so the more one will learn about listening to the magic of data the better they will cop up and upgrade in their metrics of life in the age of data it is just like learning how to mine oil although oil is not a new thing that does not make it any less cheap to learn how to mine data science ps also if you are looking for new technologies to learn better focus on genetic programming augmented and virtual realities and biotechnologies these have the potential to become new booms in the future']

Representative_Docs: ['yes you are correct technology has failed to replace the jobs it has eliminated if it had done so there would ve been no profitable point to using it why dump half a dozen sys admins for cloud of you re just going to have to rehire them as cloud admins right they said ai was eliminating jobs but it was going to make all sorts of new jobs wrong what were those jobs going to be they probably weren t ai ml engineers i can t possibly think of one thing the dumbest employee at my company could do profitably with ai barring something that involves deepfake and only fans what management doesn t really realize yet is that if they wipe out their workforce why in the world would the company need them too if you take the paradigm far enough you eliminate every single repetitive routine task to automation if you don t have busy workers any more do you need all the layers of middle management to drive them as systems become better scaled and integrated i ve always wondered what prevents absolute monopoly clearly we re seeing this play out with amazon at some point having the freedom to chose what you eat for dinner will be a luxury another point is that i think management tends to just lie to suppress revolt like if you have a team of people who s job it is to like manually copy paste data between two legacy systems that just can t be replaced for rhetorical reasons let s say you pop some screen scraping keystroke recording cursor guiding macro type automation at the task suddenly that team is redundant do you role in with it stand the server up script the events lay that team off then their manager then that persons manager and consolidate the remaining teams under whoever is left in one fell swoop how is that going to affect company moral until you can drop those humans on one hand you can reduce operational costs of said task to maybe a month cloud instance and occasional professional services to patch or upgrade and review the macros while eliminating maybe k in payroll on the other hand you ll probably witness that k go out the window when the rest of the company starts dragging their feet in protest i dunno we can t even make art anymore ai can paint draw and generate music most people aren t sophisticated enough to know the difference or even care to if the painting is pretty and nice it s good art half of popular music is just shit beats mediocre samples and auto tuned voices saying the same dumb hook over and over it wouldn t be terribly far out to produce and entire discography with gpt and some other random lstm or gans what else of most block busters are cgi plus deepfake just keeps getting better only a matter of time before we can generate believable human like movements of cgi models and start dumping full generated movies and shows out there it s all sort of a matter of conditioning we can t even tell who is and isn t real on social media anymore our windows to the world are just these little digital x screens our future is one where we are all just human goldfish milling around in a tank full of fake plastic plants for literally no purpose whatsoever', 'it s worth remembering that the topic of ai is years old the first person to suggest artificial neural networks did so in the s the topic of ai machine learning has had a huge resurgence in the past couple years but it s not a new concept it s just a concept that s become a lot more broadly applicable in recent years due to some improvements in the concepts and very importantly it s gotten far more practical to harvest tonnes of information very cheap to store it all and relatively cheap to process it all compared to decades past but after thinking about how widely used and prominent machine learning is becoming especially if you extrapolate that further down into the future there s the problem though and ai has had it before people take recent successes and improvements and imagine they ll continue and so far the historical record suggests ai tends to boom and bust there s even a special term for it even ignoring that though i think there s main things that ll keep the average programmer from having to learn much about machine learning if they don t want to current approaches even with a lot of incremental improvement are very inflexible you can train your model to recognize cat photos very well and you can even train a car to stay inside the lines and learn it shouldn t cross solid lines but may cross dashed ones you can t train a model that ll react sanely to big sudden changes and worse than that you can t train them to respond well to novel problems covid threw off models pretty effectively for example because it was a big sudden change that the models had no training for and more than that they couldn t have been trained for it without time traveling to the future to get the data needed to understand how such events effect the market human intelligence is still so so so far ahead of any ai out there in terms of the ability to get a small amount of information and make big often reasonable inferences about it with whatever other knowledge the human has a human business planner can hear about how there s a pandemic and a lot of the economy is going on hiatus and tourism s probably dead for the next year or two and that business planner can take prior knowledge and synthesize a new approach machine learning models replicate what they were trained on and you need huge amount of information to get them trained they can t really react to big changes except by being retrained on new data which takes time and of course data you may not have when a surprise pandemic hits there s a lot current machine learning can do brilliantly and there s a lot of value in the ability to recognize trends a human never would give a model ten million mris tell it which turned out to have cancer and which didn t and it ll start seeing things doctors can easily miss or notice extremely subtle patterns no human could ever hope to notice no matter how many mris they looked at but it s a very specific kind of problem even if ai gets better and becomes much more widely available why assume it ll be a programmer s job to understand it most programmers have a minimal understanding of the internet all things considered it s mainly networking experts that deal with the hard parts of keeping the internet backbones going and getting big organizations better peering arrangements most programmers couldn t do a circuit diagram to save their life i know i couldn t but that s on computer engineers not programmers we already have the concept of the data scientist which at some organizations already basically means ai people it s a different discipline to understand those systems and the industry already seems to appreciate that fact by having it as a separate title', 'ai probably won t be replacing programmers beginner or not any time soon code generation ml models like the one openai built are capable of generating only basic code and openai has a reputation for sometimes over hyping their results anyways most likely because it helps them get funding so i d take their videos with a grain of salt but even if their model could generate complex code i don t think that d be a sufficient replacement for a programmer the hardest part of programming isn t actually writing code it s with coming up with a solid specification of what to build in the first place and how to structure the code on a high level this high level design work can probably only be automated if we had full general artificial intelligence that has human level communication skills and creativity and if we had that kind of ai we d then be capable of automating pretty much all jobs not just programming however i do think it s the case that there isn t much space for beginners in the industry not due to ai but due to the libraries tools and services that other programmers are constantly making many programmers have an innate drive to automate as much as possible and basic tasks are easiest to automate and automating some basic tasks will make some previously complex tasks simpler which in turn makes them candidates for automation and as these libraries services are open sourced or turned into products there s less and less demand for people who are capable of only doing basic things for example people who want a nice looking basic website can now just use websites like squarespace and weebly rather then hiring programmers this raises the bar for people who want to make a living freelancing and building websites since now they need to be capable of building something more sophisticated then these types of website generators capable of finding customers who want something more sophisticated']

information so if you could please help me understand what the field looks like i would appreciate it gt you say you like the math and concepts of ml but the two areas where you would use that modeling for business prediction and research you say you have no interest in i have less interest in applied statistics and much more interest in the geometry of probability spaces and functional topological analysis i would actually like research were it not for academia constantly turning out counterproductive feedback loops on prestige and competition not to mention i have no illusions of actually getting paid to study whichever branch of the field catches my attention and it would end up being a form of grunt work all the same just with a research engineer aesthetic as opposed to a software data engineer aesthetic so i am happy to keep my study of the deeper material as a hobby and focus my professional aspirations on what will reward me more materially but i was hoping there was a place for me as research adjacent as someone with engineering chops to actualize the models and methods developed by a more core research team i don t know if that exists or what it would be called i m sorry if i come across as argumentative i just want to express my desires and questions as fully as possible so as to increase the odds of finding the information hardest to get from general search thank you for taking the time to reply edit having looked at the job descriptions for the titles you provided machine learning engineer is very much in line with what i was describing as per idealized occupation in case that helps clarify anything']

Name: 4_masters_master_degree_ms
Representative_Docs: ['ultimately it s up to you and the kind of job you re trying to get tl dr up front if you re looking to get a very specialized or research heavy or bleeding edge job in machine learning or something then you d probably need a masters to be competitive if you want to be a software developer devops or sre in of normal positions still doing interesting work but nothing highly theoretical or research heavy then a masters is just going to be a waste of time and money your average company hiring software engineers doesn t really care about credentials beyond your bsc and even that is optional in some cases if you have enough existing experience i ll try to touch on most of the points you go over even if you enjoy learning you need to stop sometime so that you can actually make money you can always learn on your own time outside work or find somewhere to work that has a budget to send you to conferences seminars or get continuing education in night classes being a career student isn t a good idea a masters degree will not get you a higher salary when you re just starting and it may actually exclude you from a lot of normal roles unless you specifically aren t interested in them anyway because a masters implies that normal dev work would probably bore you if you re not getting a masters now you probably wouldn t need one later in your career after you have a family because you d already have a career without one at that point a masters doesn t really make you any more employable than you already are unless it takes you more than years to decide that you hate doing normal sre dev work and you want to do theoretical or highly specialized work instead that you d need a masters for if you re worried about imposter syndrome and not learning enough out of university don t worry everybody has that your internships and classwork should be more than enough to prepare you and if you actually managed to graduate without learning enough to get you started then a masters wouldn t help you anyway the vast majority of what you d be doing in industry you learn on the job anyway your classwork only provides a basis framework employment rate is up for debate especially in tech since most tech companies already had work from home procedures in place i m not aware of any tech companies that i or my friends work at who have had to lay off devs or go into a hiring freeze due to covid my own company has multiple interns and some new full time hires telecommuting from across canada and we re still currently interviewing for new positions and interns a bsc is meant to be broad so that you have a good framework to build whatever knowledge that you might need for a specific job on top of a lot of job descriptions if you read them carefully will actually mention that a lot of those specific skill sets are only recommended and junior positions even more so a lot of those are written up by hr who will just throw everything that may be related to the role into the list if you took a semester off because you were sick i imagine you would have made those classes up at another time before you were allowed to graduate at least the mandatory ones and you would have taken other electives anyway within cs to replace those ones you missed and complete your elective graduation requirements electives just give you an introduction to parts of software development you might find interesting they re not like core competencies you re missing if graduate classes doing much more specialized theoretical topics looking interesting to you then maybe that is the way to go and you can ignore the other points i made above better than just being bored doing normal development work but it will also be a much smaller job market for whatever specialty you decide you love and you may cut off parts of the normal market for yourself by intimidating interviewers or losing your software development core competencies because you might not need them in your specialty especially research theory work', 'please take in mind i don t have practical experience but i did a lot of reading asking about the whole should i do a master s question as well so this is my summary nothing i say here can t be found for yourself on google quora but i can get you started going for a master s degree can certainly work to get your foot in the door so in that regard you should look at application requirements and see whether your application has a shot or not every master s degree is very different some will be theoretically very heavy some will be more practical for example the university of iowa states explicitly that their master s degree is designed with practicality in mind and that people who are more interested in theory should consider a phd so you need to research specific programs and if possible get in touch with current students or professors because of the nature of master s programs they are heavily dependent upon the professor that is there may literally be courses that exist at some programs that do not exist at others because one has professor with a phd in applied robotics and the other has a professor in agricultural internet of things stuff like that so the master s degree can go from high tier to mid tier in a short period of time if a few key professors leave or a new director takes over or whatnot generally speaking though exactly because of this master s degrees tend to be specialized programs one will have an awesome professor for x but mediocre at y it seems from what you ve told me that you have no idea what you want to do in the future other than program something because mobile game botanical recognition do you mean visual recognition like ai machine learning and something politically adjacent are all different fields of study so the master s degree comes down to this it will expose you to a lot of different coursework and potentially help you narrow down your decision but will also mean getting through a lot of unnecessary coursework and graph theory sort of classes you will get more out of a master s degree if you are able to really pin down what you want to do in tech anecdotally my plan right now after my transition out of my previous career is to work as a full stack web developer which i enjoy as a discipline in and of itself but am not sure i want to be my only career i am interested in machine learning robotics and ai and embedded technology as well but these are not easy to get into from a nonrelated career and i m not single focusedly passionate enough about any one of them to commit to an expensive and slow master s degree yet a job in web dev will give me a good start in the technology field and recommendations to create an application for a master s degree in the future should i desire additional or different challenges but will also set me up for a career i enjoy if i don t do the master s in short too late i would take the time to analyze your situation do you need to make a decision right this second or do you have some time to do what i did generate some at home projects and see a field that you can be happy in and then apply directly there are many jobs out there in mobile game and web politically adjacent that will not require a master s but will definitely require projects and showing what you know if you are looking at artificial intelligence and machine learning it s more likely that you will benefit from a master s degree or even a phd depending on whether you want practical ai or theoretical ai', 'sooo here i go i ll answer your questions in a list respective to the way you listed them mcs vs ms i don t see how it would be a cop out however you should be asking yourself why you d want to do one over another it s clear that the mcs is either for people who weren t satisfied with their previous experiences whether it be in academia or industry and want to strengthen their portfolio or if they just want to learn more computer science topics but do more practical work ms is clearly for people who want to get research experience and maybe go do a phd deciding which one you d like to do should be clear if you want to try research are interested in pursuing a phd are interested in jobs that require research experience or want to be a scientist then ms is the clear choice every program has its own deadline so just meet that however if you re interested in research you should be reaching out to potential supervisors prior to even applying for graduate school my school has a special program for undergraduate students who know they want to grad school they need to apply for it within their first year or two and it basically fast tracks them into graduate school on the condition that they maintain their grades so be on the lookout for special programs if you re interested in something like that that depends entirely on the company at amazon they ll pay for education if you ve worked there for at least a year or two years i forget in my experience a company would only be willing to pay for the education if you ve already been working there for a while does it look bad to drop out i mean how would an interviewer know if you dropped out unless you directly state you did i don t think it looks bad even if they do know graduate dropouts are more common than you think if the company was paying for your education now that s a different story the severe consequences that you should think about if you were to drop out is that you have wasted your own time money and effort what i wish i knew i wish i would have known to do more research in my undergraduate degree that gives you such an advantage for obviously applying to graduate school but also your experience if you already have past research experience it can help you secure funding more easily and most importantly it helps you discover what to do research in much earlier additionally i wish i took a gap year during that time you can really assess whether you want to go back to school or if you enjoy software engineering also take this with a grain of salt but i personally recommend taking a break from school graduate school can be exhausting i m in the middle of my masters right now so i can t really say if it was worth it but if you re absolutely certain that you just want to do software engineering a masters isn t necessary in fact industry experience is better however it would be worth if if there are jobs you re interested in which demand for a masters computer vision machine learning robotics cyber security etc is it worth it financially if you re paying for it it s not you lose years worth of salary and need to pay for tuition additionally a masters won t necessarily ensure that you ll make a higher salary probably very theoretical or math heavy courses anything practical you can really learn on your own but if you re interested in research especially if it s theoretical your math needs to be strong edit formatting edit i d like to add on this if your background is already in cs or software related doing a masters without research is useless in terms of career advancement based on my own research jobs that demand a graduate degree are mostly asking for what sort of research you did']

Name: 5_resume_bullet_point_remove
Representative_Docs: ['alright here goes so first and foremost i am not a recruiter but i have done hiring and looked over a lot of resumes so this is just one guys opinion in my opinion there are two stages in the thought process of a recruiter when they pick up a resume is it worth my time to look at this resume for more than seconds i have decided based off my second analysis that this resume is worth actually reading to determine if this person is qualified in my opinion your resume fails number one and if you don t pass number one you can t pass number two here s why recruiters love buzz words on your resume you have aws kuburnetes react terraform devops sql etc all super hot technologies methodologies right now impressive for a junior dev recruiters don t know what those things are but they know that they want them the problem is every single one of those buzz words are on the second page of your resume of the time they are never going to get that far i think this resume would be far stronger as a page resume how could you do that i think profile sections on resumes are distracting and overrated in most cases i think the profile section can either be condensed to sentences max or completely replaced by just putting a title next to your name on the resume student full stack engineer or something like that if you really want it put it in a cover letter or something recruiters get s of resumes a day and there s no way they are reading those walls of text i know i m not i didn t read yours i would also consider removing the casual academic position to help condense this resume whatever you have to do to get that technical skills section to the main page another great thing to have in your experience section is any quantitative metrics you can come up with how many users did you scale to what was the uptime of your project etc a side note on linkedin a few weeks ago i contacted an old friend who works at apple to get a referral she works in recruiting she went to my linkedin to check it out and then chose to give me some unwarranted advice i m glad she did my linkedin experience section basically just had a bunch of descriptions of what the company did abc is an ai company specializing in b b blah blah blah anyway she told me to plug in what i did accenting all the technologies i used basically make those buzz words super visible so i went in and wrote one brief sentence about what the product was and like a full paragraph about how the front end was built in react and what aws technologies i used basically dropping buzz words left and right and i noticed a drastic increase in recruiter emails like drastic drastic i think this advice works for experience sections on resumes as well at any rate a lot of this is just my opinion but i hope it helps good luck', 'change about me to skills education remove your percentages software engineer point saying you re employed is obvious remove that bit start with saying you developed a an app that with lt skill gt lt skill n gt point stick with one sentence per point remove the comment about you working in a team this is obvious add more details about these improvements point explain in more detail how you improved the algorithm point this doesnt demonstrate any technical skills math teacher this is irrelevant also this reads like something i would see on linkedin not on a technical resume use of st person perspective personal projects deep learning neural network just combine the first and second point so you explain what you made with what and how remove the last point we all learn i dont see results above critique applies to your other projects', 'you definitely have the qualifications to be an entry level developer i wouldn t consider that a stretch at all based on your resume your high aspirations should be faang top tier fortune types if that s what you want to do purely due to your lack of swe internships here s how i would change your resume header consider a different font for your resume i think you re using times new roman which isn t bad but it s a little tired i d make your hyperlink the same color style as the rest of your text education remove university altogether if i m reading it correctly you never achieved your economics degree so employers won t care about it at all i d probably remove it even if you did achieve your economics degree it s just not relevant to swe university just list your graduation year and not your start year pare down your relevant coursework section i d probably just leave ai intro to ee and choose one more algorithms everyone who has a cs degree has taken the rest of your courses it s redundant with i have a cs degree and makes it look like you re trying to fill space projects amp languages split this into two sections projects and skills and platforms move your car before your sever trading project add a bullet about how successful it was capstone maybe add another bullet clarifying how the vibration was used to authenticate professional experience move before projects experience after projects is for people who have relevant experience your it job is relevant squeeze any leadership bullets out of your experience that you can it job split the recovery plan assisted recovery bullet into two move these to the top reword the recovery plan bullet as led maintenance of business continuity and plan these bullets at least to me show that you were a valued skilled and trusted employee interests make this a full section choose depending on space and list them with at least bullet point each describing your involvement either with a volunteer or employment relevant slant']

Name: 8_learn_programming_web_python
Representative_Docs: ['learning to code has become more of a trend these days i mean yes this path offers a lucrative career with impressive salaries but mind you it is no easy path to walk upon there are many ways in which you can learn to be a coder like getting a cs degree takes four years joining a bootcamp takes up to months or follow the self taught route time depends on how quickly you learn each method has its own benefits and what path you choose affects the outcomes while i can t give a straight forward answer to your question i can draw a comparison between self taught and bootcamp route so you can understand what kind of choice you need to make self taught benefits this method of learning fits your schedule because it is flexible you can make your own time table and balance other work with your study routine you can maintain a lifestyle learn coding and have nice weekends with your family the next point that works in self taught favor is its affordability you would find a huge spectrum of course online some of which are available for free and some may charge a minimal fee you can begin the training as soon as you sign up with your personal e mail id you can pick your own material based on what you want to learn in a more a la carte style where you can focus on particular technologies instead of buying into specific big packages with minimal investment less commitment you can easily begin your journey to be a coder what you may not like there are some downsides of following this path to begin with the students bear the full responsibility for curating their learning material staying on track staying focused and evaluating their progress and then adjusting the approach as needed all this may not sound like much but play an important role in deciding your future you can get distracted easily and you will also need to make sure there are not many gaps in your learning process you would find it harder to network because you are formally not a part of a group and ask any coder and they would tell you how important networking is in the field it is harder to get a job on your own without any professional assistance now we will talk about the bootcamp route bootcamp benefits i myself am a bootcamp graduate so i am a little biased towards them but i will present as clearer a picture as possible without letting my love for them affect my review when you are put in a group your learning process is accelerated talking about concepts solving problems and building apps together really help you pick up the concepts quickly in a bootcamp there is more structure in your learning process since you follow a carefully designed curriculum that was made after months of research by trained and experienced professionals this method keeps you progressing faster which is impossible to replicate by yourself but you also need to keep in mind that you maintain a pace or you won t be able to keep up bootcamps are intense hence they keep you focused and dedicated towards one goal to make you a coder for a period of time you eat sleep drink and talk coding and leave everything else behind when you are studying in a bootcamp you are surrounded by like minded people who share the same goal as you learn how to code this plays an important part in your job search as you would have a professional network to leverage one of the most crucial benefits that bootcamps provide is career guidance tech is a new field for a lot of people so finding your grounding initially can be hard which is why to find better job prospects and the right tools to nail the interview you need a bootcamp what you may not like it isn t all rosy as joining a bootcamp has certain downsides that may not sit well with you foremost is the intensity which can lead you to have almost no social life forget about partying meeting friends attending social events for a span of months you cannot lose your pace or you will be left behind and maintaining focus can become stressful sometimes also bootcamps can be expensive as much as for one full time course although some camps don t charge you anything until they find you a job but then you cannot be so sure of the quality of education given there top bootcamp suggestions my suggestions are incomplete if i don t share a list of top bootcamp with you here are the best options i compiled during my search for good ones no on the list is flatiron school which is online and provides full and part time courses you have upfront payment and easy loan options and the courses are data science full stack development and web development they even have in person camps in various cities across the us next is thinkful on the list which is also an online and in person camp with full time and self paced courses they offer you month to month installment and deferred tuition options they teach data science web development ux ui design digital marketing and full stack development synergisticit is also a great option if you are looking for an immersive online only bootcamp with easy installment payment plan to providing the best job support they try and make your coding journey easier their specialty is java and they also teach some of the most in demand skills like python data science aws mern stack ai etc to experienced as well as beginning coders they have smaller batch size max candidates and also offer live classes with focus on teaching relevant material they are the best hybrid of a camp and consultancy that is out there springboard is next on the list they offer online coaching in form of full and part time classes and have ux ui design data analytics full stack development and data science courses they have self paced courses as well for students who have other commitments general assembly is the last name on the list that is an in person and online camp they teach ui ux design web development full stack development engineering immersion and digital marketing they provide an immense job support and have some of the top it companies as clients final words clearly there are a lot of factors that go into considering which method of programming you should choose the timeline of college may de motivate you if you want to begin a start up in a few months or lack of guidance can present problems for you on the self taught part if you have hard time learning without a concrete plan tl dr is something like this college good for legitimacy in job searches but bad for time efficiency and cost bootcamp good for learning skills that are in demand to get a job faster time saving networking and getting good fast bad for cost and ease for scheduling self taught good for beginners is flexible and involves low cost bad for guidance and clear structure so make a choice keeping these points into mind to ensure fruitful outcomes good luck', 'let s get to it if you re not struggling you re not learning what s your idea of learning to code whip out your computer type out a couple of cool commands and bam beauty personified sorry nope the process is at least struggling especially at the beginning and that s how it should be i ve seen people quit programming because they don t want to struggle with bugs that s like hating the gym because you don t want workouts to hurt but the struggle is the way you don t run from bugs you train yourself to find them quicker gt a proficient programmer is an efficient detective of bugs if you re feeling frustrated that things are not smooth remember that this is how learning actually works neuroscience research confirms that practice doesn t simply make you perfect it is deliberate practice to improve in a specific task that makes you better and deliberate practice involves struggling so your mantra should be you code you debug you struggle you overcome and you repeat if you ve not struggled at all you re simply playing around if you struggle all the time then the next section is for you you re not dumb you re learning at the wrong level let s start with a little analogy imagine learning the english language the language of exceptions you start with a tutorial motivating you to learn to spell big words like elephant but it s not working after beating yourself for so long you decide to go to a lower tutorial and learn to spell smaller words like ant but you still struggle so you conclude that you re dumb because you lowered your tutorial level and still didn t grasp anything but when a new teacher investigates why you re struggling they find out that you ve been struggling because you actually don t know the english alphabets let s bring this home to our coding world some people jump into beginner tutorials to learn a react framework elephant then realize it makes no what you mean by that no reason c is less accurate than python at representing data you ll just be using native types directly whereas this is abstracted away from you in python gt if java is made with c can i make a android app with c only c language you may have to start off in java can t remember but once the app is running it can call down to native code using the android ndk which lets you write c c and call it from your java wrapping types so that they are compatible etc you shouldn t though unless you need to use the language you re supposed to not the one you like gt an i make a beautiful gui using c sure there are gui libs written in c to provide widgets for ui building gtk for example which is written in c itself again will be a bit more tedious in c very procedural but will work just fine gt can i do everything that i can do with c and c depends what you mean by this if you mean when coding different languages have different syntax standard libs and paradigms there will be features of languages that aren t available in others or at least not in the same way e g objects in c if you re talking about achieving the same outcome from your programs sure these things are all turing complete langs running on the same hardware just in different or no environment s with different ways of telling your processor to do stuff a given problem can be solved in c c c java etc', 'personally i think recommending c as a first language is usually a mistake my philosophy is that the most important thing the student needs to learn in an intro course is how to problem solve using code c actively impedes this core goal in several ways it forces you to learn a lot of language specific quirks and nuances many of which are arguably design mistakes for example see the spiral rule which is needlessly confusing spending class time learning how to work around mistakes others have made seem like a poor use of time to me it forces you to spend time talking about lower level details such as raw pointers and memory management this is an unnecessary roadblock it d be more efficient to use a higher level garbage collected language where you can instead teach your student a simplified model of how a computer works and move on to focusing on more fundamental things in a shorter span of time it makes simple things like string manipulation annoying and finicky which limits the scope of what your student is able to build it forces you to defer teaching your students how to use basic data structures and algorithms since you don t have anything like variable length lists or hashmaps built into the language or generics for that matter instead it d be far more efficient to learn a more higher level language python java javascript c kotlin go whatever teach the principles of problem solving before moving on to teaching c as a second language perhaps along with a bit of assembly at this point the student should already understand how to problem solve and is ready to move on to other things such as learning how our software interacts with hardware and since they re already comfortable with programming you can cover this material in much more depth and rigor then if you had attempted to teach this during their first intro class the only times i d recommend teaching c as a first language is if the student specifically wants to specialize in embedded programming and such the student really wants to know how the computer works under the hood or is uncomfortable with building on top of black box systems thinking about computers abstractly learning c will help mildly with the former c still makes lots of simplifications and will let the student defer developing skills in the latter gt besides when it comes to performance does it even matter with modern processors and with things like cython don t you have good enough performance performance always matters the other comments have already talked about why performance is important for kernel code so let s pick another example suppose you re writing some web service using python and discover it takes machines to adequately handle all user requests then suppose you either figure out how to optimize your service or rewrite this in languages like go rust or java or whatever and discover you now need only machines which is cheaper repeat this for hundreds of services and you can end up saving quite a significant chunk of money the tradeoff is that the more lower level more primitive of a language you pick the longer it ll probably you to actually finish writing your code and if your code isn t ready you can t make any money this is obviously quite bad and if you want to write performant code it really helps if you have a strong understanding of how everything works ranging from lower level os details to networking to compilers also what if you one day want to try contributing to projects like cython yourself somebody needs to help build the tools we all use and maybe that somebody can be you as another example suppose you re writing code that does a lot of mathematical computation running large scale scientific simulations machine learning models and so forth doing this type of heavy computation in pure python is far too inefficient what people do instead is write the core computing logic in languages like c and fortran and use a python wrapper around this core code see the numpy library which is the backbone of the python scientific computing ecosystem this gives us the best of both worlds high performance in our core for c and fortran and easy orchestration and the top via python']

Representative_Docs: ['i had no prior coding experience before college and i didn t have any work experience either so i pretty much began coding in college over the summer after my freshman year i past years i ve been thinking about returning to school for a degree in cs i m right now and i have a master s in chemistry with some academic programming experience in a research setting with covid going on i figured i would spend my summer off beginning to take classes online since in person classes aren t an option for anyone at the moment i just finished taking intro to cs in c discrete math and linear algebra online at a community college in the us the fall semester is about to start up next week and i am taking c data structures and computer architecture organization in the spring i will take a course in algorithms and possibly an intro course in machine learning i am succeeding so far and i m enjoying the coursework i ve completed two hobby projects on the side showcasing different types of skills i ve picked up so far one related to another hobby of mine and one related to teaching chemistry to high school students amp x b my short term goal is to get admitted to a ms program in cs for fall i ve been looking at georgia tech s online mscs program in particular due to its rigor and price point another short term goal of mine is to land an internship next summer so i can get some hands on experience i live in the nyc area so i am hoping that i will be able to find a something relatively quickly amp x b some questions what technologies and skills should i be picking up on my spare time i am most comfortable writing code in c but i also have some previous python experience from my master s in chemistry when is the best time to apply for internships for summer i ve read that i should be comfortable with material in ds a for tech interviews but i won t have both of these courses completed until the end of spring again i work full time as a high school teacher so the internship needs to be during the summer how should i structure my resume in the best way possible to stand out to hiring managers i obviously don t have any industry experience so when i apply for an internship i m just going to have some coursework and side projects to show if you are currently a swe what would you do differently in preparing for a career in industry amp x b thank you all for your time', 'steps to getting a research internship from the empowerly team who the heck are you i am an empowerly counselor who earned my ph d from stanford university in cellular amp molecular biology in the past i ve mentored students working on their research projects for science competitions and have served on the admissions committee of stanford s pre collegiate summer institutes hence i wanted to share a post from the perspective of the person who would be reading your email step prepare your resume one of the key factors in getting an internship is if you have some skills that could help the lab for example data analysis skills for life science labs that conduct experiments which yield a lot of data that needs to be analyzed remember to highlight your skills in your resume and if applicable provide a link to an online portfolio such as a website or a github repository there are countless resume templates try to make a resume that is clean and minimalistic not colorful attach your resume as a pdf to prevent any formatting issues step draft an email template professors get a lot of emails and there is a good chance that they have received countless emails from high school students asking to work in their labs if you want them to read your email you need to show that you have not sent a mass email by making it clear that you have looked into their research also you should attach your resume this is cold emailing and the response rate is usually poor but you never know a tip from one of my students ask questions about the professor s research and what specifically interests you in the email here is a template for the email that you can edit to make specific to you dr my name is and i am currently a in high school i am emailing you to ask if you would consider me for an unpaid internship in your laboratory i am interested in the intersection between medicine healthcare neuroscience machine learning ai etc and hypothesis driven laboratory research and i plan to go into career e g healthcare in the future it is for this reason that i would be very excited to have the opportunity to contribute to your laboratory s research in on i am particularly interested in last summer i i was introduced to subject topic in my class where i learned how to i really want to explore this subject further by being able to conduct research first hand i have attached my resume at the bottom of this email please feel free to contact me if you have any questions or would like any additional information thank you for your time and consideration of my request sincerely step research professors and be able to fill in the template with specific details about their research remember the more specific you are the greater your chances are of a professor accepting you always try to add your own skills and what you have learned that you can bring to the table despite what you might think you learn a lot in your high school classes that at least give you the baseline skills to conduct lab research step realistic expectations getting research opportunities is difficult at all levels and extraordinarily difficult at the high school level thus do not be dissuaded by hundreds of negative responses by professors in fact in all likelihood you may not even receive a response the key to getting research as a high school student is persistence never quit and you may very well need to reach out to hundreds of professors in order to have success']

effective without an audience agrees the covid crisis has forced us to reconsider some of our assumptions about the world maybe it s time we also reconsider our understanding of good teaching i write from the perspective of having taught law for more than a quarter century and having both taken an online college class about years ago and taught one last spring ask your friends to tell you about their best teachers they likely will describe people who were passionate about their fields and student learning knew their subjects cold communicated great faith in students capabilities and insisted Name: 16_math_calculus_algebra_need
Representative_Docs: ['a good math background doesn t hurt and it may lead you to think about problems more algebraically that being said you don t need much of a math background at all for general programming if you just want to work in webdev desktop application development etc then you can probably get away with only knowing basic operations and modulus if you want to work in game dev then a little more math is likely required at least a basic concept of linear algebra and some geometry if you want to work in data analysis or machine learning then there is a higher math requirement again a solid understanding of linear algebra lots of statistics probability distributions etc and some calculus tldr don t let your math skill be a major factor in learning programming unless you want to work in some specific industries a pretty basic math level will suffice', 'did you just suggest that calculus trig and algebra could be learned in or months easily because i m pretty sure people spend years learning these subjects normally and still just under of college students who take calculus already ambitious students fail it i m not saying it can t be learned by the vast vast majority of people but it s pretty important to give it lots of time if you don t you re not going to learn retain it calculus and most advanced algebra trig is all about analytical skills you can get those through practice but give yourself time and don t be frustrated if answers approaches aren t immediately obvious i just worked on a project that required some intermediate ish algebra to get the algorithm the solution itself was quite obvious but a lot of members of my team simply didn t see it because the approach to get the the solution was not obvious at all it required solving two sets of three equations in three variables and comparing the results but that s not really what it looked like on the surface if your math skills aren t great rest assured that you can improve them give them time most coding isn t math intensive but mathematical logic is coding logic computer science is really a weird branch of applied mathematics at its core so being a decent mathematician does help if you want to work in data science ai ml you ll need a pretty good grasp of linear algebra some calc based statistics and finite mathematics game designers may need some calculus and analytical trigonometry to make the physics of their games realistic all coders benefit from understanding discrete math proofs and the detective skills that allow you to solve equations that require obscure trigonometric identities for example if you re not a very rare math genius don t try to go from intermediate algebra to multivariate calculus in two months you d be setting yourself up for disappointment and reinforcing everything you think about yourself not being good at math i think you are good at math you just need more practice and a better grasp of the foundational rules that make things like infinitesimals im possible to think about', 'from the r learnprogramming faq do i have to be good at math not necessarily programming and computer science is a wide and broad field and whether or not you need to be good at math depends on what discipline you chose to specialize in for example some disciplines of programming do require strong math skills d and d games will make frequent use of vectors matrices and linear algebra data analysis and machine learning make use of many concepts from statistics and calculus and working out algorithmic complexity is easier if you have a good grasp on algebra and basic calculus in general the more advanced and complex the topic the more likely it is that you need to have a strong mathematical foundation if your goal is to be able to work on those types of complex problems then having a good foundation will be invaluable however just as many aspects of computer science and programming do not require a good understanding of math the only things you really need to know are how to add subtract multiply and divide and maybe a little basic algebra many programmers go through their entire career with having to do barely any math at all in particular many product facing roles e g web development mobile development require very minimal knowledge of math in those sub disciplines what matters most is your ability to write robust and maintainable code as well as your ability to figure out how to build things that are valuable to your users in any case if you re not very good at math you can definitely learn and improve after all you re presumably not very good at programming yet either but here you are learning to code check out khan academy and you can find free lessons starting from basic math on up do note however that the answer is a bit different if you are interested in getting a degree in computer science many universities or colleges will have specific prerequisites you need to fulfill before you can either enter the major or graduate calculus classes etc and since computer science is to a certain degree applied mathematics you will almost certainly end up learning about discrete math writing proofs and other similar topics while earning your degree you do not need to be inherently good at math in order to learn these topics or any mathematical topic really but having some mathematical maturity certainly wouldn t hurt and would probably smooth the learning process exactly how mathematically focused a cs degree will be differs from school to school some schools place a heavy emphasis on covering theory while others de emphasize both in favor of focusing on application and some schools emphasize both if you want to find out more you should directly investigate the degree requirements and structure of whatever colleges or universities you are interested in attending we will not be able to easily predict what direction some given university will skew to summarize you do not necessarily need to be good at math if you want a successful career as a developer if you want to be a computer scientist you will end up learning a fair amount of math if you want to work on harder problems within certain domains math can be a helpful tool depending on the situation']

Name: 17_ml_demand_ai_people
Representative_Docs: ['i have a few years experience as a research scientist in computer vision for medical applications my observations of related ai ml dl ds field is that a lot of it from companies both startups and larger companies is mostly for signaling purposes for large tech companies like google and nvidia it is very good marketing for tell everyone how much money you save in data center using ai or how self driving cars will be here in years the media investors students love it and it helps them sell more cloud services or gpus amp x b yes there are generally breakthroughs in dl that have useful application but it is not concordant with the level of publicity and people wanting to do ai the real benefits come from incremental improvements in performance in areas such as recommendations demand forecasting visual search speech processing machine translation image recognition volume segmentation anomaly detection etc that filter through services you already use or benefit from like watching movies online or spam detection companies that actually benefit from these improvements in capability have large amounts of data resources and technical capacity to develop and label models as well as buy in from management otherwise it is mostly vanity ai projects to boost some manager executive s career or raise valuations in fundraising at a startup amp x b as for the other two categories bi and analytics in my honest opinion bi mostly exists to help executives feel better about themselves or provide convenient justification for decisions they ve already made or were going to do anyways similar to management strategy consulting it is getting easier to make simple dashboards and i see this getting absorbed into other job functions or largely automated by fewer analysts analytics and data science is also becoming more accessible to wider technical audience why you see so many statisticians economists physicists geographers etc rebranding retraining as data scientists more rigorous thinking about statistics and data to inform decisions is a good thing but it s hard see demand for data scientists analysts ever catching up with supply of new graduates career changers unless the entire revenue generation revolves around data there will usually only need a couple data people for a team or even division in each company universities are more promoting and profiting by offering masters degrees and even bachelors degrees in data science i think its becoming harder to obtain one of those positions unless you are exceptionally talented or have some specialized domain knowledge that combines with data science in areas such as gis law medicine operations research actuary etc', 'in the past months years i have noticed that the mood in the data science community is changing it seems as if every project is doomed to failure one could also get the impression that almost no ml engineer or data scientist is working with real machine learning i want to point out that it is not as bad as it seems in this subreddit i have been working in this field since before the hype started and it is true that there have been exaggerations especially in the media an incredible number of things were promised that could not be kept suddenly machine learning was to be used anywhere where a little thinking was the solution however very exciting ai systems have been built which also provide great value there are successful start ups that have achieved really big things with ml yes and there are the data scientists ml engineers who have created value in the company with neural networks and also really do a lot of machine leaning aside form simple regression xgb it is not like at university where new algorithms are invented and research is done much of it is craftsmanship and plumbing but still machine learning is often the core of such systems i personally was involved with several ml system a lot of them still in production and generating value i think another reason for the shift is that there was an inflation of data science job titles from people entering the field with months bootcamps i don t want to gate keep but you can not expect to play on the same level as people with several years exp phds masters self taught whatever don t take job titles to serious i also don t want to make false promises there is more supply than demand for ai jobs a lot of people entered the field for the money however if you are passionate about ai and ml and did your studies people will notice there might be a lot of supply but
