Name: 97_students_humanities_college_social
Representative_Docs: ['gt useless multicultural classes useless history requirements so on and so forth there s a little less than full semesters worth of crap at most schools and that s only the completely useless crap well if you really actually think learning history is useless and reject the concept of educating your entire mind instead of just producing just another tech worker that speaks volumes about you second focusing purely on raw career benefits because apparently that s all you care about believe it or not communication writing and social skills are critical to your advancement hopes pretty much everywhere i found liberal arts classes have allowed me to develop invaluable career skills such as learning how to moderate a discussion how to write good documentation how to communicate points effectively how to present and speak publicly how to appreciate and analyze opposing viewpoints and so on i ve found that people who took these classes for granted tend to have completely garbage skills outside of raw technical skills with sometimes catastrophic results to their careers i don t think that s unrelated gt there s the additionally useless crap for software developers like physics chemistry etc i won t count math because at some point even the most esoteric math can be helpful you know considering the amount of work and money these days involved in developing scientific and robotics applications you know grail raised nearly b it reeks of short sightedness to think that somehow software engineers just will never use physics or chemistry or any science i myself have previously worked at an industrial analytics company where my science knowledge proved invaluable unless you can completely predict the future you have absolutely no idea if any knowledge will be useless in the future or not and that s the benefit of a holistic education you have the background and tools needed to adapt to pretty much anything i also think knowing more about the physical world is beneficial to me as a person but what do i know gt then there s the multitude of classes that may have been useful maybe depending on your career path but very unlikely for most people to be useful how exactly do you know what your career path will be unless you actually take the classes and find out some things for yourself how do you know you don t want to go into hardware or ai or scientific computing or computational linguistics or hardcore databases or compilers language analysis or academia the first generation of data scientists were people who took ml classes something that was extremely unlikely for most people to be useful according to people like you at the time and now they re raking in boatloads of money maybe they should ve taken your advice instead and ditched college eh not to mention all the people that did the distributed computing concentration years ago that are just swimming in cash now something that people in industry were often completely clueless about back then the point is if you didn t want to take advantage of the knowledge in universities that s really on you and most people if you actually want to talk about most people most people are terrible at self teaching so they especially need the enforced scheduling curriculum and rigor of school to properly but somehow i think by most people you really just mean yourself gt e g when was the last time you needed to prove a language wasn t regular using the pumping lemma see this is what i mean you think the purpose of proving a language wasn t regular using the pumping lemma is to just teach you how to prove a language wasn t regular using the pumping lemma no it s to teach you the properties of a regular language and have you really understand what that means it s supposed to get you to internalize the nature of programming languages and be able to reason about them what they do and don t do i use that understanding of programming languages very frequently by the same measure i ve also never wrote any os or systems code but it turns that my os and systems classes have been invaluable to teach me how computers work in reality believe it or not actually understanding the tools we use is pretty useful in this career not to mention that all those algos and proofs really improved my problem solving and analysis skills all these kids can spend hundreds of hours grinding leetcode my one algo analysis class allowed me to skip of that gt there s a very tiny section of college material that is actually useful on the job and in my case i d learned half of it before i went to college on the job believe it or not there s more than one job out there also believe it or not you yourself might work multiple kinds of jobs in your lifetime ok maybe not you since you seem to not care about this possibility but probably normal people as for myself the diversity of topics i was educated on not only told me what i didn t like it set me up to explore other careers if i wished my old ml interests are now bearing fruit all the stuff i learned in my econ classes have resulted in me actually being good at analytics so i get to basically be the pm for my team too maybe i ll switch to pm full time maybe i ll go back into game dev when i near retirement who knows well i know who doesn t know people who neglect a diversity of learning but here s something that i do use everyday in my life a better understanding of society the world the tools i use the work that i do and the people around me i gained that understanding by learning about a lot of different things and by learning how to think critically so if you don t think that understanding is useful if you define the benefit of college as literally the technical knowledge you get to use day to day on your current job that speaks more about you than the education i mean even the most career oriented people tend will acknowledge the power of the networking that college can get you assuming you ever took advantage of it', 'our attorney told us the bad news yesterday earnestness she says is falling out of favor the only thing that she thinks has any real potential anymore is playing off of specialization maybe even an artistic specialization but that s not an easy pivot for me i ve been working towards shotgun pre professional for the past eight years with the standard empathy complexes not quite full shotgun but a balanced set of time sinks generic enough but not too generic the type that usually gets safe adjusted yields across the board year after year but my silver bullet was supposed to be earnestness and it would have worked great in the past but this year the rumors really are true with the huge increases in applicants using asset protection colleges are having second thoughts about the effectiveness of their earnestness evaluations the latest simulations show a massive reversal in the qualities which the applicant classifiers select for earnestness is projected to go down by why is my portfolio so earnestness focused to tell you the truth it s my grandparents fault the years they applied to college some sixty years ago colleges began their shift to the current system discarding primitive metrics like the sat and applicant essays in favor of more comprehensive black box solutions that was the same year that the college board announced its partnership with google colleges began using applicant classifiers deep learning models which college admissions officers adjusted year to year by filing information requests the public slowly uncovered the hyperparameters which the models looked for in applicants there was academic potential creative intent technical all of the metrics which are discussed intensely today on college forums and discourse channels and of course there was earnestness the big data version of the college board s old adversity score like the adversity score it measured how much an applicant deserved their academic success but it used massive amounts of data to do so your zip code your family income the crime rate in your neighborhood but also your social media posts your fitness data your religious and political activity the college board believed that the previous adversity score had failed because it hadn t given a complete enough picture of people s lives using more data they reasoned could help earnestness succeed where the adversity score had failed my grandparents came from upper middle class families they hadn t put a lot of thought into the application process expecting their hard work and passion to shine through naturally like so many others in that first generation they saw their own college applications ruined by low earnestness scores they didn t get into their top choices on my mom s side my grandmother didn t get into any college and they all suffered greatly living off of unemployment and working odd jobs through the recessions in the s it was tough but they made it through scarred by their experiences both sides of my family drilled the importance of improving earnestness into their kids from the moment they were born even at that time some thirty years ago they were the rare families who got college counselors focused on personal experience and not on test scores my own parents went to the right public schools worked the right blue collar jobs mcdonalds home depot rejected the right internships they shunned private school kids and rich neighborhoods even as their families started to make decent money my parents and their counselors established all of the classic earnestness tricks we use today on my dad s side my grandparents paid for an emotive experience off the charts even by today s standards spending a summer in the alaskan wilderness going fishing and reading thoreau my mom spent time on a farm where she could tend to the goats both of their social media accounts were stripped down all of their photos delisted or filtered so as to be unreadable by the college web trawlers my parents took dna tests tracked down missing relatives with possible hereditary conditions my mom s family found a second cousin who had died from lung cancer it all boosted my parents earnestness scores made them perfectly imperfect to the applicant classifiers you couldn t study for the earnestness evaluations but you could still cheat it those counselors played the game perfectly for my parents and they set the scene for the flourishing asset protection industry we know today my grandparents love talking about how they were the early adopters how they learned from their own mistakes and brought my parents great success they tell me the story about how the asset protection industry got its name back in the early days families of college applicants would hire asset protection attorneys versed in financial law to hide records of their wealth so that colleges would think they had less money than they actually did that way they could receive more financial aid from the college that is part of what asset protection does today but instead of just hiding money asset protection attorneys today hide everything that might affect our earnestness scores instead of reading fafsa and financial aid policy they study the human legible parts of classifier patterns to help us create authentic portfolios they help us pretend to live the imperfect lives that colleges look for our family has a high carbohydrate diet with unhealthy amounts of junk food we live in the outskirts of a logging town which we moved to after eighth grade my parents work remote our family has few books mostly romance and pulp fiction logged online i pretend to sneak woolf and shakespeare late at night after i wash dishes the biometrics pick it up the books that i really like must wait for car rides when the bumps prevent accurate scans my attorney and parents dictate my friendships ethnically diverse low middle class with small but loveable quirks i alternate my time between the classes at the community college my part time job at a restaurant and my scouting troop each appear to have been found through my own initiative but in reality they are the result of months of my parents and attorneys planning over encrypted channels although my parents are millionaires i walk to school and to work everyday i have never broken a promise missed a meeting or failed to arrive early to an interview my parents trusted in earnestness it s like a religion to them it worked for them my dad who got into tufts and my mom who got into a uc sc say cliched stuff like the market optimizes for authenticity earnestness isn t a strategy it s a way of life they paid some hundred grand for our asset protection attorneys they think that it s a kind of magic but they re out of touch and the latest changes to the classifiers prove it they re squeamish about even the shortest college lists the hyperspecialized kids who apply to just schools who have been doing applied quantum computing since they were born those kids they say are trouble whose parents work at amazon and gave them privileged cloud access with their first tablets but the facts are that their portfolios are high yield even if they are risky the thing is all things considered my spread is average it s not like i have to fill out my applications manually my college list is simply a matter of applying the right filters i m going to be applying to about applications maybe if i need to flare out my safeties and the distribution of schools that i m applying for aren t the bottom of the barrel either i have the uc scs in my reaches i have a good number of state schools i might even apply to tufts itself if only because my parents still think legacy counts for something i just wish my parents had given me the option to choose to live a life that wasn t perfectly imperfect take a vacation somewhere really nice to spend money and not have to hide it in off shore bank accounts to forget about earnestness for a second the college application process is a game no matter the quality we optimize for it s always been a game and i m getting tired of playing it', 'edit i appreciated all the comments despite the condescending tone and as someone else pointed out obnoxiousness of my original writing many of you wrote lengthy responses to explain the issues that my post has to me from the perspective of the social humanities faculty i will keep these in mind as i work with my colleagues i keep my original post here to warn myself again writing too much about disciplines that i thought i know about but it turns out that i don t x b x b original in the light of the recent post about humanities tenured lines being cut off i just want to say that as a cs faculty sometimes i feel that my colleagues in social and humanities are a bit passive in the face of changes personally i highly value the quality that a well balanced education bring to our students cs for me in this case i tell all my students that they are probably going to learn similar technical skills and what makes them stand out is their general and social courses i point out that unless they become a cs researcher professor it is most likely that they will bring their cs skills to work in a non cs environment and that their domain knowledge however elementary it is will set them apart from others with similar cs skills but without the domain knowledge i push my students to go for high quality non cs courses rather than just grade padding that being said i felt that the tools approaches not the theoretical contents being used and taught in these courses are extremely behind and to be honest uninspiring take introductory writing courses for example none of my students after taking the writing courses come back and be able to do things like knowing how to use google scholar how to build automatic citations using software like words endnote or mendeley now their writings themselves remain horrible and i understand that it takes a lot time and practice for writings to improve then why can t we teach students to leverage technology to write more quicker and faster can t there be assignments where students create and maintain online blogs discussing their work or their learning process god forbid we want students to learn latex which will set them on a path for a research career regarding upper level courses as a cs faculty i wish that i could have more collaborators from social and humanities who want to do computational x computational linguistics computational sociology computational political science you name it in fact you create a course in any social sciences add a dash of statistics and a pinch of data analytics visualization and your department is on the path to join the hottest current trend of data science i taught intro to programming courses to non cs students and i have the upper level students talk about their work and data and i yearn at all the potential data science engineering work that could be explored with these interesting relevant data reach out to us cs in particular i implore you edit i want to set a better context for my post i am currently tt at a public pui institution and i previously worked at an r institution as an it computational support staff as i mentioned in a comment below my observations on students perhaps not quite applicable to r r this is not to say that social humanities faculty here are more reactive to change but it says more about the quality and caliber of preparations that r r students typically have at smaller state universities where students need more support and hand holding and need to do more to compete with their peers from higher ranking institutions in job hunting students are more likely to be pragmatic and focused more on stem disciplines this therefore becomes a simple calculation for the administration instead of maintaining lines of tt for a social humanities department that does not have a lot of enrollment fewer demand on upper level courses they could spend that money on more tt line for stem discipline and hire several adjuncts to support low level social humanities courses for gen ed requirements more enrollment more likelihood that students will get jobs with higher salary upon graduation what s not to like about this to survive and to grow therefore requires adaptation from social humanities faculty i also want to make the following comment on my own behalf cs faculty are not facing the risk of being cut down even at small institutions unless the entire institution is at risk therefore cs faculty have no incentive in reaching out as u purrprinthom suggests my own incentive comes from my background because i love seeing how cs technologies can be applied to advance research in other disciplines and i also highly value a well rounded education as stated above few others cs faculty are like me and while they are open to collaboration they are unlikely to reach out']

Name: 99_matlab_python_anaconda_package
Representative_Docs: ['functions are a little tedious to write in matlab making a gui is a hassle too unless you pay more for another toolbox that s the thing with matlab if you want more functionality you have to pay more on top of the base license signal processing more money statistics more money machine learning more money image processing more money so if you will to pay for capabilities it can be good but if you don t have those toolboxes life in matlab becomes a lot more difficult don t get me wrong there are tons of really useful toolboxes the mathworks has to offer i fiddled with a lot of them while i was doing my undergrad one thing that really was annoying though is how simulink models are not backwards compatible unless you go and save them for use in previous versions that caused a whole lot of trouble when professors were using an older version of matlab and students were using the most recent version under the student license python is open source so anyone can write tools and libraries so there are tons and tons of resources that people have already gone through the trouble of writing', 'gt python being extremely slow is not a concern for beginners just add to this python being slow isn t really a concern for real programmers either because the speed of the python interpreter doesn t actually matter for most applications for heavy duty numerical code you re going to be using libraries anyway for example even in c you d use fftw instead of rolling your own fourier transform and pyfftw is available for python for other numerical things you d use numpy scipy which runs code compiled from c and or fortran and vectorize your code for record oriented big data things you re going to be using hadoop spark your choices there are python java and scala and r kinda again the speed of the python interpreter has little bearing on how fast your code runs java scala will be a little faster for some udfs but the difference isn t huge imho the python spark bindings don t feel as clean as the scala bindings but speed isn t a big issue for ml ai stuff you re going to be calling into some framework like pytorch or tensorflow to run on a gpu speed of the python interpreter again doesn t matter for anything involving a network interpreted code is not the bottleneck just look at the popularity of node and react honestly even r which has a justified reputation for being slow and a memory hog can be pretty fast if you know what you re doing the main trap for slow r and python code is trying to write c code in python r don t do that python can be used for just about anything but i wouldn t recommend it for writing device drivers systems programming and embedded but not because of speed', 'individual components of the toolchain probably exist but i m not aware of any equivalent toolchain matlab and simulink together is the industry standard each separately is very good at what they do but together they are essentially unassailable unseating it is extremely hard because anything else will need to put in a tremendous effort to build a similar integrated toolchain and then benchmark it on real systems which seeing as no one wants to risk their expensive equipment to save a couple thousand bucks there really is a massive dirth of test cases that could actually be used besides that yes technically i suppose there is no reason you couldn t do it in python but the issue you run into there is that no one really wants to deal with the relative indexing because all the literature is one relative cause you know math also numpy is clunky and cluttered notationally making it very hard to read and compare to the math references you will be using matlab having matrices as the core data type means your notation is very similiar to the math and thus easier to read and compare something like julia might be able to bring enough other advantages to the table though without the numpy clutter to actually eventually make it worth switching but you would still need the toolchain and in particular a simulink clone is a tall order additionally besides all that matlab has a tremendous amount of data rich toolboxes like the aerospace toolbox that makes everything extremely productive that would need to be replaced and the data validated again not cheap really the place where matlab fell behind python and exposed a chink in their armor was a roughly year lag in machine learning that they are working hard to close but i m not sure they will be able to catch up as tensorflow and friends have established themselves and are the tools where the research itself is being done so matlab always feels a little lagged this precise effect exists in reverse in the controls industry and makes it unlikely that anything will be able to overtake it in the foreseeable future']

Name: 100_math_mathematics_degree mathematics_bachelor degree
Representative_Docs: ['it s way too early to know how well you would do as a mathematics researcher i m an applied mathematics phd student myself and let me tell you that mathematics is not filled with elusive geniuses it is absolutely correct that raw intellect is not sufficient to be good at mathematics research discipline rigor and hard work go a very very long way it s hard to overstate this mathematics is also not really a young man s game unless by young you mean under and you will likely barely be up to date on modern mathematical knowledge in your field by the time you reach your mid twenties the notion that then you do your most important work is totally unfounded and incorrect i believe you vastly underestimate how deep every single subfield of mathematis has become nowadays and how long it takes to be able to contribute anything meaningful to it there is also no advent of artificial intelligence to speak of machine learning is first and foremost a product of applied mathematics and it is not ai by any intuitive sense of that word and it has enormous systematic flaws which need to be worked out by mathematicians or discovered to not be resolvable and alternative approaches found etc may human mathematics eventually become obsolete yeah but before then almost every other job will become obselete mathematics is not under any threat whatsoever you may have bought into significantly too much media hype about what is essentially a series of unstable optimization methods they produce spectacular results when used by clever people in specific ways but they are far far off anything useful for general purposes i work on such things and with people who work on such things mathematics is safe', 'i have heard that a bachelor s degree in mathematics can lead to some really decent jobs like programming and data science i am just curious to know how realistic it is to get hired as part of these jobs with only a bachelor s degree in mathematics is it even possible also is it true that a bachelor s degree in mathematics helps in becoming a better software engineer does anyone know any advice', 'i have heard that a bachelor s degree in mathematics can lead to some really decent jobs like programming and data science i am just curious to know how realistic it is to get hired as part of these jobs with only a bachelor s degree in mathematics is it even possible also is it true that a bachelor s degree in mathematics helps in becoming a better software engineer does anyone know any advice i understand that computer science degree is the smart option but i really love mathematics more i am just interested in seeing how useful this degree is to get hired as a software engineer or data scientist i don t go to a school that is well known i don Name: 104_memory_material_chunks_chunk
Representative_Docs: ['hello all i would like to ask for any hints or information on how to take the most of my months window time a little of context im a years experienced senior software engineer with focus in computer graphics and real time rendering i managed to take months to study statistics and datascience related subjects so i can try to switch to data science area i need to take the most of my time so i would appreciate any hints on how to divide allocate the time in a day week month optimally e g study only one topic for a while then switch or allocate time for n subjects per day i need to study several topics project based seems a good idea so any hints on how to manage personal short timed projects to learn a specific subject how to measure progress and make sure i m not just wasting my time maybe put myself out of my comfort zone by joining some online challenges how many hours per day how to balance rest study so to absorve information optimally thanks', 'i finished a m in software engineering while working full time as a java developer got the job right after college the same time i started the master s during the regular curriculum i only attended the labs which were over pm or in the morning at am so attending the laboratories consumed like a maximum of because sometime i skipped classes hours per week and some of the homeworks were done in weekends the only thing that is left now is the dissertation now that i do not have a strict schedule and i can make my own schedule i am trying to find a better strategy to balance time my thesis is in the strategy to allocate like hours after work for work at the dissertation or to compress all the dissertation work for weekends like h per weekend day the main advantage of working over the weekends would be that you do not loose momentum in hour i enter the rhythm i think you can be more productive from this side the disadvantage would be that the motivation may be lower because during the work week there that vibe of everyone is working i know this is a poor excuse then there is the problem of getting rid of the stress accumulated during the work week usually i use the whole sunday for this will it be effective to work h my boss forces me to do overtime now if not i will be fired a day from monday to friday and allocate hours for detaching activities will this get rid of the need for a whole sunday to detach stress i know it depends', 'i read a mind for numbers by barbara oakley because i want to get better at working with numbers and learning the book was really helpful and i wish i had this when i was in college wanted to share some of my notes here and recommend that you consider reading this book if you want to improve how you study and learn these are some of my notes my favorite quotes those who are the most disagreeable tend to be the most creative this is because creative people challenge existing answers and assumptions merely glancing at the solution to a problem and thinking you truly know it yourself is one of the most common illusions of competence in learning the dread of doing a task uses up more time and energy than doing a task itself procrastination can be like taking tiny amounts of poison it may not seem harmful at the time but the long term effects can be very damaging one might say that work substitutes for talent or better yet that work creates talent simple explanations are possible for almost any concept no matter how complex key questions how can you avoid falling into the trap of thinking that quicker people are automatically more clever can you find ways to turn your disadvantages into advantages general notes two modes of thinking focused mode diffused mode when you procrastinate when studying you are leaving yourself only enough time to do superficial focused mode learning the resulting neural patterns will be faint and fragmented and will quickly disappear use the focused mode to first start grappling with concepts and problems in math and science after you ve done your first hard focused work allow the diffuse mode to takeover relax and do something different those who are the most disagreeable tend to be the most creative this is because creative people challenge existing answers and assumptions focused attention can often help solve problems but it can also create problems by blocking your ability to see new solutions one of the first steps towards gaining expertise in math and science is to create conceptual chunks mental leaps that unite separate bits of information through meaning basic steps to forming a chunk step focus your attention on the information you want to chunk no television on in the background don t look at your phone etc step understand the basic idea you are trying to chunk step gaining context so you see not just how but also when to use this chunk when learning a new concept look at sample problems with worked out solutions one disadvantage of looking at worked out examples to form chunks is that it can be easy to focus too much on why an individual step works and not on the connection between steps when you first begin to learn something you are making new neural patterns and connecting them with pre existing neural patterns that are spread through many areas of your brain if you chunk without understanding it is a useless chunk that won t fit in with other material you are learning learning takes place in two ways a bottom up chunking process practice and repetition help you build and strengthen each chunk so you can easily gain access to it when needed a top down big picture process it allows you to see where what you are learning fits in context is where bottom up and top down learning meet chunking how to use a problem solving technique context when to use that technique instead of some other technique illusions of competence and the importance of recall attempting to recall the material you have learned retrieval practice is far more effective than simply re reading the material many students experience illusions of competence when they are studying when you have the book open in front of you it provides the illusion that the material is already in your brain you don t want to wait too long for the retrieval practice try to recall the material you ve learned within a day if at all possible re write your notes in the evening after a lecture once you have something down you can expand the time between upkeep repetitions to weeks or months getting a concept in class versus being able to apply it to solving a genuine physical problem is the difference between a student and a full blown scientist or engineer your brain has roughly four spots available in working memory when you are first chunking a concept it takes up all four spots once a concept is chunked it takes up only one slot in working memory merely glancing at the solution to a problem and thinking you truly know it yourself is one of the most common illusions of competence in learning pick a mathematical or scientific concept from your notes read it over then look away and see what you can recall working toward understanding what you are recalling at the same time then glance back re read the concept and try again the ability to combine chunks in novel ways underlies much of historical innovation reference the book where good ideas come from by steven johnson the difference between creative scientists and technically competent but non imaginative ones is their breadth of interest the bigger your chunked mental library the more easily you will be able to solve problems as you gain more experience in chunking the chunks you are able to create become bigger two ways to solve problems through sequential step by step reasoning focused mode through more holistic intuition creative diffused mode in building a chunked library you are training your brain to recognize not only a specific problem but different types and classes of problems so that you can automatically know how to quickly solve whatever you encounter you will start to see patterns that simplify problem solving for you the retrieval process itself enhances deep learning and helps us begin forming chunks it can help to recall material when you are in a physically different location than where you learned the material doing something physically active is especially helping when you are trying to grasp a key idea interleaving versus overlearning interleaving means practice by doing a mixture of different kinds of problems requiring different strategies mastering a new subject means learning to select and use the proper technique for a problem you learn this by practicing problems that require different techniques to solve them emphasize interleaving instead of overlearning overlearning means continuing to study or practice a problem immediately after some criterion has been achieved for example correctly solving a certain type of math problem and then immediately solving several more problems of the same kind overlearning provides diminishing returns techniques for limited study time read but don t yet solve assigned homework and practice exams review lecture notes rework example problems presented in lecture notes work assigned homework and practice exam questions chunking verses choking how to increase your expertise and reduce anxiety this section discusses how to enhance and refine chunks steps to building a powerful chunk work a key problem all the way through on paper do another repetition of the problem paying attention to the key processes take a break gives your diffuse mode time to internalize the problem sleep do another repetition add a new problem repeat steps on the second problem do active repetitions mentally review while doing something active testing in itself is a powerful learning experience it changes and adds to what you know also making dramatic improvements in your ability to retain the material this is known as the testing effect build a mental solution library a key to building mental flexibility and expertise is to build your library of chunked solution patterns summary of chunking chunking means integrating a concept into one smoothly connected neural thought pattern chunking helps increase the amount of working memory you have available building a chunked library of concepts and solutions helps build intuition in problem solving when building a chunked library it is important to keep deliberate focus on the toughest concepts overcoming procrastination we develop a passion for what we are good at it is a mistake to think that if we are not good at something we do not have and can never develop a passion for it keep a planner journal so you can easily track when you reach your goals and observe what does and doesn t work write your planned tasks out the night before arrange your work into a series of small challenges don t blame all your problems on external factors enhancing your memory we have outstanding visual and spatial memory systems the memory palace technique placing memorable nudges in a scene that is familiar to you allows you to dip into the strength of your visual memory system see this article on litemind for more information learning to use your memory in a more disciplined yet creative manner helps you learn to focus your attention by memorizing material you understand you can internalize the material in a profound way one of the best things you can do to remember and understand concepts in math and science is to create a metaphor or analogy for them use spaced repetition to help lodge ideas in memory create meaningful groups that simplify the material you are trying to remember if you are memorizing something commonly used search online to see if someone has already come up with a particularly memorable memory trick beware of mistaking a memory trick for actual knowledge there is a direct connection between your hand and your brain the act of organizing and re writing your notes is essential to breaking down large amounts of information into smaller digestible chunks writing and saying what you are trying to learn seems to enhance retention several recent studies have shown that regular exercise can make a substantive improvement in your memory and learning ability the rest of my summary is here']

Name: 106_research_vision_computer vision_industry
Representative_Docs: ['gt what is the research job market like in the private sector it s there but really limited to either startups who have some big bright idea and might be lucky and have a university and some vc funding or big corporations with an r amp d department and the budget to support it the former is risky your chances of being made redundant fired and the company being declared bankrupt is higher but if it succeeds it will probably be bought out by some large company deepmind xnor ai etc corporates are slow and whilst researchers can have some autonomy from the internal bureaucracy it still gets in the way most corporations with r amp d capability have it to try and get a competitive edge in their products to make more money and or create technology and license it out patents when the money gets really tight advertising and r amp d expenditure are the first two budgets to get cut gt are the jobs more abundant i have no connection to academia so i can t answer that there are certainly less roles in research in the private sector than everyday software engineer positions in my career i ve seen more researchers move over into the writing boring production you want to do both then i would recommend starting with javascript for a couple of reasons javascript is the language of the web javascript is supported by all browsers there is a huge community with vast amounts of resource available to learn and ask questions of javascript can be used for both server side and client side application development this allows for the full stack to be written in the same language javascript can be written in object oriented or functional paradigms based on what you are trying to achieve so you aren t locked into specific programming paradigm there is even a Name: 116_grading_grade_points_graded
Representative_Docs: ['think of the following scenario you have just graded papers and on the th you decide that maybe this particular error is more common and want to give back a couple points to those who made that exact same error in normal grading you have to go search back through those to figure first who made the error did you mark off points and how much in gradescope change the markdown for that issue on this th paper and it retroactively applies it to anyone who made that error before and then move on second scenario you accidentally inconsistently take points off after hours of grading then the whining begins in gradescope can t happen everyone making that mistakes get the same points off third scenario the same error in a written math problem happens all the time and you want to give partial credit for it mark the first one and let the ai determine who else made that same mistake the same way and automatically assign the points that is the part the ta s love', 'how much different is that from a regular grading system if something is turned in hastily written hard to read not stapled and still has the spiral edges on it its going to get anchored low before even looking at the content ideally this wouldn t happen but having only a human grade stuff does not inherently make the process pristine i m not advocating for ai grading because as this is not an application that ai is good ready for i just want to push back a bit on the thought that grading is a pure objective process when it by its very nature is massively subjective no matter the subject', 'my point was that because grading is inherently a very subjective process the anchoring through ai throws an additional wrench into the factor it is not a defence of grading as a practice as it currently exists though i fail to see a different method that would be truly revolutionary in the first place if grading was a pure objective process it wouldn t make sense for me to argue about the concept of anchoring and its negative effects on grading would it how much different is that from a regular grading system if something is turned in hastily written hard to read not stapled and still has the spiral edges on it its going to get anchored low before even looking at the content the general game is far more understood here while obviously no system is perfect there is a general understanding of what passes the muster and what we should aim for everything you mentioned is typically understood as things not to do in comparison ai systems are far too rudimentary to be able to present a clear and understandable objective that wouldn t also be simultaneously easy to game at least from what i ve seen and we see this in op s claim that the auto grader failed to understand some Name: 121_interviews_hirevue_interviewing_interview
Representative_Docs: ['i m not commenting after this because quite honestly i feel like you re over complicating the interviewing process by trying to perfect it it doesn t have to be perfect you can interview specialist and generalists via the process i specified hell the only difference i would add is maybe allowing generalized approaches if they re unfamiliar with the stack or allowing them to show up how they might do the ui too if it s for a front end person you can leave customizable endpoints to these questions the problems shown to interviewers can be one you solved a long time ago working with whatever piece of the software you so choose but ensuring it isn t a current problem they need to solve regardless my point is job postings post for specific individuals backend engineer front end full stack embedded firmware ios android mobile cloud native ml data science the list goes on and it is idiotic to treat all those interviews the same because what you need to know and what is important to those domains is vastly different putting a junior level leetcode problem to filter people is fine but companies need to independently acknowledge what s important to them for who they hire and interview off those principals i could say upfront for my company the ability to work on cloud services is a big one so you should be expected in an interview how to integrate those services into a new piece of functionality and if that s too specific then to architect that piece with correctly specifying your domains for these various potential services and how they might connect event message driven or an exposed endpoint trade offs just seems so much easier to stop over complicating the interview problem by mindlessly following the leetcode pattern maybe that works at big companies because you re hired extremely generally and typically hire younger engineers in bulk however there is plenty of general programming knowledge and patterns you learn in time and domain expertise that should absolutely be leveraged in the process because what you re getting are interviews which indirectly scope what they re looking for', 'i ve edited what was once a pleasant response into more of a rant but it s something i need to get off my chest for the most part my interviews have been just wordsmithing shit that my interviewers want to hear for data analytics and nlp advanced crud operations for sql or explaining the word vector it s analogous to having a race car driver applicant talk car mechanic stuff because that s what the interviewer was interested in it boils down to it s fucking hard to create a good interview so most interviewers test solutions they deeply understand solutions they care about it s not ideal but it s fair enough beats doing a two week free consultation disguised as an interview what we have in the software industry is like the restaurant industry come in cook a foundational dish that all chefs in that specialty should know how to cook and you pass but this does not work well in software as today we make biscuits tomorrow we make pizza so most interviewees spend time leetcoding or googling all possible interview questions create meaningless sizzle pop portfolios or spend years on a degree learning what would soon be outdated material gt all to please the almighty interviewer because in software nobody cares if you can bake the world s best souffle if you can t even make a good biscuit i once overheard people at major faang company ask what the interview questions were i had major in my head why should admission to a company be about interview questions rather than talent hard work and a portfolio or in the case of junior engineers just passion loyalty and cheap pay this is why i advocate that software should be more like construction no one asks how you put in a nail in construction they hire you watch you and make the decision on the job imagine if software interviews were like construction here help us do the next sprint i think a good business model would be to create such cloud platforms for new on board contractors to work for a few days to months without coming in contact with corporate ip to all the interviewers out there just because professionals with years of experience are able to pass the interviews don t mean the interviews are a good thing it screens out a lot of cheaper motivated workers and unproven talent it even screens out professionals sometimes because the real professionals answers are lost in all the noise by applicants who simply prepared for and googled your questions i think most interviewers know this deep down but do interviews as a way of checking boxes i noticed that the higher up the interviewer is in the chain of command the less they conduct standard tests but ask more to figure out how much value the candidate really has', 'i m not commenting after this because quite honestly i feel like you re over complicating the interviewing process by trying to perfect it i m a little puzzled by what you re trying to say here the original question more or less boiled down to why does everybody seem to do interviews suboptimally and my answer is every single method of hiring we have so far is suboptimal in some way are you disagreeing with my conclusion or disagreeing with the idea that it could be valuable to try and find a more optimal approach and it is idiotic to treat all those interviews the same because what you need to know and what is important to those domains is vastly different yes as i said earlier most competent companies do indeed customize their interview loops if they re looking for somebody with expertise on a specific domain or if they re looking for somebody junior vs senior but as with all interviewing strategies the one you proposed is also also imperfect because it still takes non trivial amounts of time and energy and therefore money to design a new high quality interviewing loop and even if you do invest time and energy you can still end up with something that leaves interviewees unhappy for example take op s original post where he she mentions being asked unrealistic statistics questions presumably those questions were a part of some company s attempts to create a specialized data science hiring loop and op wasn t satisfied with their attempts similarly creating a good set of how would you design x on a high level let s discuss tradeoffs problem takes time and energy not everybody is looking for specialists not really a pro or a con but not everybody is good enough to clear whatever the initial baseline hurdle which means they falsely get the impression that some companies do not specialize their interviewing loops when in reality they do their perception is skewed you could make things a bit easier for yourself by adjusting your loop to be easier ask things like can you do standard task x this works great for companies that just need somebody who can do whatever x is or other basic tasks but many companies have more ambitious dreams which for better or for worse ends up pushing up the difficulty of their interviewing loop beyond what s currently required for the specific job at hand just seems so much easier to stop over complicating the interview problem by mindlessly following the leetcode pattern i think over complicating is the wrong word to use here for better or for worse it s more work more complicated to create n different interviewing loops then it is to just make or more work more complicated to try and innovate over just copy catting what other people are doing it s a trade off standardizing mimicking will simplify life for them internally at the cost of decreasing things like the accuracy of their hiring process external perceptions of their company by interviewees maybe lowering the successful job offer rate it sucks that this is how the incentives work out but that s life']

Name: 125_engineering_se_software engineering_software
Representative_Docs: ['getting one or the other is not that different if your aim is to be a software developer but it will change depending what your interests are if you want to get a job as a developer for a company or startup it s better to focus on learning tools we are using nowadays like back end frameworks django front end frameworks react angular etc docker git etc improve your problem solving skills with codewars or hackerrank since it will help you in interviews most of the time we don t deal with complex algorithmic problems and constantly keep learning the weird parts of your language and possibly pick a new one to see what it changes if your field of interest is machine learning video games ai then go for a computer science degree since you ll get a formal background on algorithms statistics and math if you want to make software for devices that are not regular computers and phones and possibly make your own device then go for computer engineering since you ll get a more formal background on electronic engineering now i m saying it s not that different since part of being a developer is constantly learning new stuff and even complex stuff like concrete math you may be able to learn on your own in less time than it would take you college when learning it with a project or job in hand tl dr computer science will give you a deeper background on algorithms and data structures computer engineering will give you a deeper background on electronic engineering but keep in mind knowing this will only matter if your field of interest requires it having good problem solving skills being comfortable with tools used nowadays for production code constantly learning things about your preferred programming language and picking new ones will give you better job opportunities', 'hey so i m currently an incoming freshman studying cs this fall at my university and i discovered that the cs department at my university actually offers both a cs degree and a software engineering degree the difference while cs goes over lots of theory and is very math based the se program offers a more practical approach to cs and actually goes over things like building different types of software project management etc the degree plans for both majors are exactly the same however save for six classes se drops a few of the math and theory classes in favor of courses focused in software testing software project planning software architecture and three classes in a specialization that you choose in the se degree the specialization tracks are information assurance embedded systems computer imaging ai human computer interaction networks thats basically the gist of the differences between the degrees again it s not much of a difference because in total there are only six different classes between the two degree plans but being a builder and someone who likes to create apps and different programs i m a bit leaning towards switching to the se program which offers a more practical use of cs i m also not as adept at math so se sounds better to me my university also doesn t allow us to double major in the two as they are way too similar which would have been a good choice if it were allowed my problem is if the traditional cs degree is preferred in the professional world would doing the se degree put me at a disadvantage when applying for internships and jobs would i have to explain my degree to employers do large companies prefer the more well known cs degree keep in mind that both degrees follow the same exact degree plan albeit with a difference of six classes in total i ll still be taking the important classes like data structures and algorithms with the se degree', 'hey i m a th year computer science student at university of manitoba your question is actually quite complicated let me explain broadly speaking computer science is the mathematics of computation software engineering borrows the useful bits of computer science and applies them to the real world to solve problems you can think of it as being the same relationship between something like physics and mechanical engineering now at most canadian universities a software engineering major is generally part of the engineering department while a computer science major is generally part of the science department at the u of m specifically this is where things get very confusing there is a software engineering specialization not major in the computer science degree but also in the computer engineering degree what are the differences well if you specialization in software engineering through the computer engineering degree you re going to learn a lot more about the hardware side of things and you re also going to be doing a ton of electrical engineering courses this degree is also fully accredited by the professional engineer council so you ll be able to legally call yourself an engineer and you get a ring the title p eng added to your name if you get the software engineering specialization through the cs degree you re going to get a more traditional education a focus on the software side of things like algorithms data structures etc and the ability to take some courses like artificial intelligence machine learning cryptography etc keep in mind software engineering is a specialization and not a major the majors you can pick are computer science or computer engineering personally specializing in software engineering through the cs degree is most likely going to be far easier and regardless of the path you choose you ll be working at the same jobs so i picked the cs degree for that very reason i don t care about calling myself a p eng or having a ring either summary software engineering at the u of m is a specialization in both cs and ce pick ce if you want to call yourself an actual engineer and focus on the hardware electrical engineering side of things pick cs if you want a traditional education with more of a focus on software cs will be easier and regardless of what you pick you will be working the same jobs also regardless of what you choose make sure you try and get into the co op program it s fantastic co ops are essentially paid internships and you can work for some pretty big companies google and amazon routinely recruit students through this and we also have ubisoft setting up a headquarters here and they are recruiting heavily i m in the co op program myself but have only worked locally interviews are extremely easy not very technical at all and there are generally far more job positions than students so if you get accepted into the co op program you re pretty much guaranteed an internship']

Name: 132_gpu_cpu_multithreading_tpu
Representative_Docs: ['a friend of mine gave me a pretty nice old gaming rig of his it s got a core ryzen and a gefoce gtx in it the problem is i don t really do any pc gaming so i haven t really gotten much use out of the gpu he mentioned that it might be useful for doing some machine learning stuff with python and that that was something very employable right now i realize python is really well liked but so far it hasn t really appealed to me that much i dislike the syntax and the dependency on whitespace i have more experience with c and like its syntax and independence on white space much more but i m also not afraid to admit that i think some of the concepts that might be involved with parallel programming on a gpu might go way over my head especially in a language like c i m wondering if there s anything i can still do to take advantage of the gpu with c or maybe there s another language too look at i haven t really looked that much into machine learning it s basically ai isn t it so still thinking that maybe there s an area that it can be applied that would appeal to me and make me more keen to learn python cryptography appeals to me so i feel like there s probably some areas where i could apply it towards that so yeah anyway just kind of trying to find something to take advantage of this hardware and that might be kind of prudent and maybe lead to gainful employment as a secondary effect', 'pretty close the cpu is general purpose and designed to handle a wide variety of tasks intel makes a variety of them i i i etc with four to eight cpu cores to do the work a gpu has a more limited set of instructions and they re focused on graphics operation like shading d calculations and texture mapping and a gpu may have hundreds of processors the tpu is a google thing designed for ai and machine learning you re not likely to find one in a home pc', 'multithreading is complex so is how processors work multiple cores won t help much if the application running on them does not allow the work to be split up by spreading it over multiple cores this is why many games you ll see mostly use one core in addition it s quite common for the cpu to not even be the bottleneck in the first place putting in effort to multithread the application when it s in fact the gpu who s the bottleneck common in triple a games is a waste of time and could even make the application slower to add to that multithreading can make performance unpredictable every now and then the information the threads are dealing with needs to synch up having to wait x milliseconds on that can make framerates choppy so no he s not completely wrong at all a lot of gamers try to pretend they know what they re talking about while knowing literally nothing on the subject multithreading is very often not at all a way to speed up a game it can help if a the cpu is a bottleneck in the first place and b the work can actually be divided over threads in a way that will actually give a benefit an example of this would be to have the ai like pathfinding run on a separate thread']

Name: 134_ll_yada_cs_events
Representative_Docs: ['gt is it possible to learn programming or software development without enrolling in college yup but you should be very serious about it amp x b gt if so where should i start what are good references most beginner tutorials are fine python is a good beginner friendly language but it also has widespread use in the industry it s one of the most popular languages in the world i got a solid book and followed this course amp x b gt is it better to get back to college college helps you learn all the stuff that is hard to learn on your own calculus discrete maths algorithm analysis amp design compilers distributed systems networking operating systems machine learning probability statistics ai you can go look at a university website to see what you re missing out on if you want to pursue serious hardcore cs work you need these foundational subjects if you want to just build amp maintain web apps which comprise a hefty chunk of the cs job market some employers found that self learning bootcamp is sufficient if you want a career in a non coding role but with tech exposure self learning bootcamp might make sense amp x b gt are there ways to incorporate architecture into peogramming or jobs that include both architecture and software development there are some startups out there that are architecture related tech companies search for them domain experience is really important', 'commercial development is a completely different beast to academic learning you ll likely spend your first year of your career having a lot of academic ideals beaten out of you and learning how commercial development is actually done commercial development has budgets deadlines and resourcing issues you ll be working a part of larger teams sharing and working on each other s code you ll be a cog in a much larger wheel i started programming at the age of or i love programming and i didn t really have to work too hard in my cs degree i thought i knew it all and then i got my first professional job i learnt more in that first year than i did in the previous years of self and academic learning i m not years into my career and am a manager of a development team and also responsible for hiring almost without fail every junior we hire straight from school almost goes into shock in their first year however any good company will have a mentoring system for their new hires to help guide them through that year and pretty much everyone i see that makes it through that first year usually come out as much stronger programmers depending on the area of work you go into i promise you ll likely forget of what you ve learnt at school but you ll learn a whole bunch of stuff that you were never even taught at school you re learning a wide breadth of cs knowledge at school to give you the best chance of finding something to work on in industry once you get a job you ll only be concerned with the stuff you need for that job so naturally unused knowledge and skills will fall away but the skills and knowledge you do use will be deeper and sharper re machine learning if you feel you don t have the level of education to apply for a machine learning position then apply anyway what s the worst that ll happen you ll get rejected or you ll get an interview you ll fail even if you do you re getting interview experience which is invaluable if you can t get a direct machine learning position then just find another position trust me the hardest thing you ll ever do in your career is get that first position once you re in a job you ll start building a professional network and whether we like it or not that s the way you ll find future positions and there ll be less emphasis on recruitment managers and interview processes interviewing won t go away but a recommendation from an existing employee can go a long way for a candidate stick with it finish school and then work on your career treat those first couple of years in your first job as extended education and learn what it means to develop commercially and professionally and then go from there it s a marathon not a sprint edit formatting', 'here are a few things i wish i was told i learned them the hard way and of course take it all with the grain of salt this is stuff i wish i knew did but it may or may not apply to you figure out what type of cs you want to do front end back end web dev app yada yada i personally did this by just doing different things make small apps games experiments don t be afraid to try to break fundamental rules in computer science every good script started off as a weird prototype do personal projects but plan them out be it solo or with a team always plan out your projects try to stick to your scope and if you can t adapt and adjust the plan accordingly a mistake i made in undergrad was picking up projects but not prioritizing project management whether you re a project lead or a developer knowing project management can help you in managing your workload professor projects you could always join a professor s projects to help with not all professors have a team of researchers who help them in stuff listen to professors as they talk about their side projects and see if they re recruiting a team for said project this can be a hit or miss and some schools support it while others may not don t feel like code is the only thing you have to do with cs while a degree does put you down a path it doesn t mean your locked on rails a cs degree opens up a lot of technical doors but it also opens up other opportunities as well an engineer can make one hell of a team leader take breaks burnout is real i learned that hard way in the indie game industry be sure to not just learn but learn to relax during university depending on where you go post uni burnout is more prevalent than others knowing how to unplug unwind and calm down after working will save you a lot of stress don t over specialize i know i m contradicting a little this list isn t perfect take classes in branches of cs you find interesting even if they re outside your focus maybe you re interested in a course on encryption maybe you re fascinated by digital forensics maybe you feel like learning machine learning try new things they may end up becoming part of your core i wish i did this during my undergrad degree one of the fun classes i took in graduate school on social network analysis has become my bread and butter in data engineering network talk to people be social and just be curious just don t go card collecting rather than hand out and collect cards sit down and actually talk for a while if you have a plan for what you want to do don t hesitate to learn about your local or wider industry and run with your plan there are meetup events more virtual ones these days that you can attend if you don t have a plan just go to events you find interesting code jam networking events on campus recruiting events yada yada most schools keep a list of these things somewhere on campus or online don t become complacent the main point says it all regardless keep pursuing knowledge you may stumble into your career have a hard time or shoot for the stars nothing is never easy but thinking you know all you need to know will make everything harder']
