Name: 136_topic_thesis_research_tips
Representative_Docs: ['hey everyone first i d like to say as a first semester master s student starting in september i highly appreciate this subreddit as it has given me a peek into a relatively closed culture i have not been a part of yet and has really opened my eyes to the dos and don ts of grad school on that note i am interested in hearing how you guys have come across up your thesis topics as a new student it s rather daunting to imagine i would have to choose a topic less than a year from now i do have my interests i do have some research experience as an undergrad but exactly due to that i am also aware of how vast and difficult to navigate the research landscape is for a newcomer for the record my master s is in math with a focus on probability theory in ai finance and overall data driven decision making as such i would like to know did you already have a topic prior to the commencement of your degree if not was your topic suggested to you by an advisor if not did you come up with it through some inspiration gained in either courses independent literature reading communication with researchers in the field for first year m sc students do you have have you received any general advice on how to spot appropriate thesis topics what happens if a student is unable to pick a topic before the end of the first year of their m sc finally how important is the topic for the quality of the thesis thank you', 'hi i am currently in a masters programm for it management and should start my master thesis soon but the problem is my thesis skills are abhorrent my overall grades are great nothing worse than a b but for my bachelors i just barely made it tbh part of the problem was little motivation due to bad bland topic choice and also lack of time i work full time now i definitly don t want to repeat the mistake on topic choice but i am just bad at finding a topic to pic that is thesis worthy i tried to go for proposed topics by teachers but my school offers very little in that aspect i wrote to some professors of my favorite subjects about topics but none replied i did a ton of reading already i know overall areas that interest me f e sentiment analysis opinion mining process mining requirements engineering cognitive computing ai frameworks business process mgmt but when it comes to the question on what to do with these topics i m all out of ideas the chances of writing a thesis in cooperation with my company is small and i d prefer to keep it seperate i also looked at other company proposals for inspiration and nothing really caught my eye doing it with another company would be difficult for legal and time reasons i know my problem is not the scientific method i can cite and write when i m not rambling on mobile and i helped a few of my friends in those aspects finding a topic is my biggest concern coupled with the fact that i tend to freeze in oral exams when i know i m bullshitting i become so anxious to say something wrong that i don t say anything at all working on it but it s only a problem in exam settings so sorry for rambling do you have any tips on how to find a topic or examples what kind of methods could be applied to topics to get an idea', 'hi there i can try to help out this is a skill that i was told by people i was good at coming up with new ideas can seem daunting after all what makes you so special that you would think of something that no one else could come up with and that is a high bar that you should not expect yourself to meet like every single skill in life it takes practice people that seem to be good at it and can do it on a whim are ones that have spent a lot of time doing it you have not yet and that s ok there are other skills that you spent your time on and have mastered so now you can spend time on this one so how do you train creativity for the most part science is not about some grand discovery that no one else saw coming yes in history books it seems that way but really those moments are few and far between and a person making a huge discovery in the field is usually just in front of a dozen others that have contributed portions you already know this so don t look for places to come up with big ideas that are revolutionary that s going to lead to constant failure and no progress start with small contributions next gain expertise in the subject matter again people are generally all alike we get the same training read the same papers think in more or less the same way if we don t know something we try to answer the same questions the only way to start inroads into undiscovered knowledge is to be familiar with what has already been discovered you don t need to be an expert on everything but you should be familiar with the general lay of the land for the field this will give you two advantages one you will not repeat the same things that others have done two you will see how people in the field approach the problems this is why reading the methods section of papers is crucial when you are learning something new next practice your critical thinking science can seem cruel but it s objective the best way to come up with good ideas and test them is to know how your assumptions and biases can lead to failure the easiest way of learning this without failing yourself for a long time is to see where others fail join a journal club make sure it s a journal club that is critical and doesn t just regurgitate the coolest papers but takes the time to analyze mistakes any time you read a paper try to come up with or ways that the methods could have been improved take your time and do it for yourself pretend that you are reviewer and you need to come up with some things for the authors to fix just so they don t feel like it was too easy this will be hard at first but as you gain knowledge become familiar with the way the field works you will be seeing things left and right it s impossible to control test for everything as you start seeing inadequacies in others work think of ways that you would address them that right there is creativity now the hard part learn from other fields i just spend a whole paragraph telling you about mastering your subfield and now you have to learn even more i have found this to be one of the single best ways to get better at generating new and interesting ideas the human brain is designed to find patterns the more disparate knowledge you have the more likely you are to find ways to combine and integrate it for example ai research utilizes neural networks which seem revolutionary in the computer science field years ago but these architectures were a well known mechanism for computation in neuroscience for years before that it s not as simple as taking what s known and just applying it to your field but it lets your brain look for answers in different ways than just the ones that everyone is doing then just practice practice practice you are in grad school because you are still learning take the time to do that and as i ve mentioned before don t look at generating amazing novel ideas the way you know you are doing very well is if you think of something and then find out that that idea had been published only years prior it means that you have caught up to current knowledge and one of your next ideas will be a few months years ahead of your peers']

Name: 137_school_schools_matter_cs schools
Representative_Docs: ['the rankings have been fairly consistent over the years harvard isn t the best at everything and definitely not the best at computer science but the brand name recognition is enough to convince most people otherwise uw madison is world class cs education many schools without big names excel at specific subjects for example rhode island school of design or university of rochestor s eastman school of music for cs georgia tech university of texas at austin university of washington and university of illinois at urbana champaign are all higher ranked than harvard the staff at many of these universities are full of professors from top or cs programs and each of these universities may be well known for specific branches or research areas in computer science so artificial intelligence computer vision algorithms machine learning systems programming language design and other areas may be better suited at some universities over others cs is a large field look at arxiv org and see all the latest research papers for all branches of cs the list of categories is not small', 'i m a first generation college student i applied to every top school for computer science the whole gamut from stanford to harvard excluding mit because i didn t have enough confidence haha i was rejected from every single school i cared about and deferred from princeton the one school i hadn t heard about i ended up choosing georgia tech in hindsight it is a fantastic school with world class education in ai and computer science but high school me had no idea when i return to my home state of new york and talk with my friends who went to the ivies mit etc they are all still figuring things out some want to go to med school some work for high tech companies top secret government contractors and some still are searching my school didn t determine any of that i couldn t have known my effort in college determined that with a bit of luck now i have a job at a top tech company and am doing the kind of ai research i have always dreamed of pursuing had i gone to another school i likely wouldn t have obtained the kind of opportunities people and experiences that helped me get my job research friends and career trajectory all the people i envied in high school for going to slightly more prestigious universities ended up in the same lattice as me the people with more talent but lower university rank still makes far more money ultimately it s your work career aptitude opportunity and choices that determine your future sprinkle a bit of luck and your school choice will hardly matter for business school it is a bit more relevant but getting into a good mba does not depend on your undergrad as much as how well you network and achieve good luck', 'if you are good enough the school you go to generally won t matter my team is not gonna dismiss a new graduate who has done extensive nlp research at school has good internships and smashed all the interview rounds it s usually pretty evident when a new grad applicant has a strong programming background and relevant experience vs one with some non substantial projects internships that are used as filler i ve worked at a large enterprise company think cisco esque but not cisco and faang companies and there are many people who come from no name colleges while it does matter ultimately it s what you can do and again it shows even if you might not be aware of it i m sure there are people from your school at top companies and even if you don t get your dream job right out of college all of it is moot with some experience and interview prep one of my online friends i did mock interviews with that i actually met on this sub got offers from amazon msft and oracle with only an associates degree and years experience at a no name non tech firm in a flyover state he went from struggling with lc easies to finishing around questions and he crushed most of his interviews this was during covid as well also the average stanford or berkeley student is not likely to have a weaker resume than an average student from a no name school even if the college names were not on the resume people from these schools and survived the cs programs of these schools generally are not there by accident and did not bs their way to a degree now add the strong signal that they ve worked their asses into some of the most competitive programs in the country and that s a very strong signal that not only did the person receive a top notch education but they re very hardworking and quick learners as well i m not saying that the school you go to does not matter even from a name drop standpoint many companies at least in the bay area receive hundreds of applications for a single hiring req it s not easy to filter good candidates from hundreds of applications and it s a generally safe bet to give those who went to top schools first shot but your school s reputation is ultimately not what s gating you from a good job it s your experience and what you can do']

Representative_Docs: ['hello i m aware that questions regarding changing advisors has been asked frequently on this community and i ve also asked a few of my own previously but after doing some Name: 140_math_calculus_linear_algebra
Representative_Docs: ['first you probably want to ask yourself what field of artificial intelligence you are interested in is it in stuffs like words and speech natural language processing is it in vision computer vision is it in robots robotics is it in graphics is it in security is it in biology computational biology etc amp x b but in general i would try to get a strong foundation in probability statistics differential equations which will require knowledge of calc beforehand linear algebra mostly on matrices complexity theory i think knowing those major opens up many doors in computer science and it doesn t hurt to study more math generally much of the higher end stuff in computer science is all mathematics having a strong foundation in math and probability is of great help in understanding these topics', 'ai at least insofar as it is dominated by neural machine learning is more about having a strong grasp of linear algebra and calculus than about data structures most of the pioneers in the field have degrees in math or physics not just cs assuming that you ll pick up at a minimum calculus through differential equations linear algebra and probability theory i would suggest beefing it up with a relatively basic course on statistics one or two on algorithms linear programming numerical methods and tensor analysis maybe switch to applied mathematics i ve heard that a couple topics from quantum mechanics are really useful depending on what coursework your school offers you can take gamut of foundational work intro to ml cv nlp and rl there are a ton of different ways to work in machine learning not all of them are nearly that math heavy if you want to do basic research in some kind of lab you re going to need a graduate degree and publications if you just want to throw some small models together or deploy pre trained models as part of a larger product you don t need anything super fancy', 'what is your objective for learning artificial intelligence if your purpose is to get a job in ai you need a phd if your purpose is to get a fundamental idea of ai you can take cs introduction to computer science then a data structures course then cs introduction to artificial intelligence with python amp x b if your purpose is to understand much of the concepts of ai you should have knowledge in basic programming ability to be able to read pseudo code python is a good language for this probability statistics calculus i ii iii linear algebra basic ordinary partial differential equations and maybe some knowledge in complexity theory if you want to study at the frontiers of artificial intelligence then you will have to study modern abstract algebra and topology also but those are for higher end even by research paper standards amp x Name: 141_phd_letters_phd program_phd programs
Representative_Docs: ['hey everyone i m a rising senior in college and i ve started to get the inevitable panic attacks about grad school i m interested in applying for phd programs in computational linguistics but i m coming from a nontraditional path so i ve been getting a lot more uncomfortable the more i prepare i m currently a double major in philosophy and history of mathematics with a double minor in comparative literature and classics this is a result of being in a great books program everyone takes the same classes here and graduates with the same major and while this gives me a lot of strengths in academia i feel like everything i m doing here is slightly off track enough to the point where i wouldn t be a competitive applicant some examples we have extensive writing requirements i had to churn out over pages worth of papers this year but they re mainly analytic papers on books that we read during our time here my last big paper was on mathematics in kant s critique of pure reason but i feel like i d make a fool of myself submitting that as a writing sample compared to someone who was submitting a published linguistics cs research paper we have zero research opportunities here but a central theme in our math science classes seems to be the evolution of scientific method and we spend a lot of time discussing things like what does it mean for something to be proved or what are the implications of empirical research etc this give me strengths in certain respects but once again i m completely in the dark about how this stands up to someone with actual experience in a lab but the idea of the two being compared definitely gives me the irrational fear of being denied with a haha you don t even have proof that you ve ever done a vertical electrophoresis in a lab coat get out normie we had a philosophy of language and formal logic sequence in our sophomore language class but the readings were literally all aquinas and augustine talking about how we perceive the word god and us singing plainchants that helped monks in the middle ages memorize valid logical moods like it s a fun story but really how am i even supposed to explain this other than this the only thing that we read that s actually relevant is wittgenstein we take two electives here i spent my last one on computation we played with a digicomp for a few minutes read some papers by turing and wolfram and then i wrote a pg paper on why people conspiracy theorize about artificial intelligence i guess this translates to some work in discrete mathematics low level programming and then ethics in tech development and i m trying to get a linear algebra or analysis one on the list for next year this is obviously no substitute for an actual cs class sequence but i feel like it s the most relevant thing i ve done thus far that said there are also a couple things that i feel like i ve been doing right i talked to an nlp person at a neighboring university and they told me to go do calc up to multivariable some linear algebra and an introductory cs course sequence i m dual enrolled at a local cc and on track to complete all these courses by the time i graduate i have to write a pg senior essay and defend it i m planning to write mine either on hjelmslev s prolegomena to a theory of language or saussure s course in general ling which should hopefully demonstrate that i a am acquainted with fundamental concepts in theoretical linguistics and b can write a thing gre prep so much gre prep i m fairly confident in what i want to focus in and have narrowed it down to a specific field or two i m interested in computational applications in language typology and statistical language models of highly agglutinated and or polysynthetic languages these seem to have a lot of overlap i care about the research i ve done reading about it and i have personal stake in it bc there are a couple endangered languages in my family heritage whose survival could benefit so much with support from modern language technology that said it sucks that everyone who writes on stuff like this are all either in turkey or siberia and i m pretty much illegal in both of those places lmao it s big trans hours up in hereeeeeee a contingency plan to get a second bachelor s in a quantitative field if i don t get into grad school on the first try an awareness that i want to do a phd for myself and not just because it gives me clout and a potential job in academia i ultimately want to be in industry anyway what can i do to make myself a better admissions candidate i feel like i have a pretty good grasp on utilizing and marketing my undergrad education but are there any gaping holes in my preparation that i should remedy i know that internships lab experience in linguistics particularly comp ling outside of home academic institutions are pretty far and few between for undergrads so i m not really sure what i should be estimating the expectation to be or more importantly let s say that i do get into a phd program i ve been told that i d most likely be admitted for the sake of academic background diversity what should i be doing to make sure that i don t get rekt by first quals tl dr undergrad doesn t know whether she has imposter syndrome or is actually trying to pose philosophical essays as research experience', 'hi everyone i was wondering whether to post his here as it sounded like an admissions question but figured that this subreddit may be better as i m under the impression that there may be more people who have gone down the phd academic researcher path or not and may be able to provide some perspective i m an mscs student who does research in machine learning and deep learning natural language processing to be more specific i just finished my second semester around a month ago my original plan was to apply to phd programs in the us and canada this year i m from korea and if things went well then i d start right Name: 142_cuny_college_degree_completed
Representative_Docs: ['so this is a genuine question not trying to pick on one stance or the other without hearing a few perspectives i go to a relatively good and reputable university and have been studying data science with a focus in ai for undergrad this required a lot of math classes and some grad courses in all honesty even prior to covid i wouldn t learn from lectures and learned to skip class self learn through books asking for direct help from teachers peers or online when needed and that has worked for me since i started coding and using stackoverflow tbh now i know ai is a deep field but in all honesty it is like any other subject mostly just books papers and taking part in building research projects internships well i can do the self studying bit already and have actually spent this past quarantine going back to the foundations mostly math to keep my knowledge fresh and i currently intern as a researcher albeit at a university since covid cancellations happened given the above how relevant is college if i can get i suppose certified is the word from companies directly build a portfolio and get references from previous research projects covid put a break in my college path in the middle of my third year i have years left and that is what is pulling me to finish i guess also that i want the option of going to graduate school which seems to require undergraduate at the same time college is unreasonably expensive especially if i am paying to be learning similarly as any other online institution most of which are free this has been on my mind for a few days so i appreciate any insight ps i do not want to go down the academia ai route of a phd and becoming a full scaled researcher the idea is to build some things and apply it to business finance or healthcare or some other industry i find interesting edit me has a genuine question wants feedback during uncertain times some user downvote i don t get this site at times smh', 'my degree has taught me everything i wanted to know about computers how they work and how they got to where they are excluding the detailed hardware aspect i went to college for cs because i wanted to understand things like how dns resolution works and what ai really is and what it isn t the first year and a half or so of college was more about programming sure but not in a context that would be immediately useful in industry just basic exercises to build my programming ability i currently do full stack web and android dev part time while i finish my degree and i learned how to use all of those tools either on the clock at work or on my own time it s definitely helpful to have a cs degree when it comes to solving tricky problems but i don t feel like i draw on anything i learned in the last ish years of college in order to do my job i do feel like spending a few grand on a week long android ios or web dev boot camp would have benefited me more at work than taking a particular cs course maybe you feel like your knowledge is superficial because most of the latest frameworks especially in web tend to abstract a lot of the technical stuff away that s why when strange problems arise with lower level parts of a project you can feel very unsure of what you re doing you never have to learn that stuff to get an app to work and usually these issues are things a more experienced senior dev will deal with anyways at least in my case while you re still new to that stuff then again i also don t know what you re familiar with or how long you ve been programming i ve been programming for just about years and i feel like i ve only just became a decent developer in the last year it also just takes time to get over the complex learning curve perhaps try working with a different language like c c and try to build a basic web server if my assumption that you use more higher level web frameworks is correct i feel like that will help you round out the parts of your programming abilities that you feel are superficial you really have to get down in the weeds with that stuff and it makes you a better programmer but again cs programming just like how construction science is not using a hammer perhaps a lackluster example one is the philosophy and the other is the tool i feel that most industry hirers only really want you to know the tool they already pay somebody more to know the philosophy if you want to be that guy then go for a cs degree', 'it focuses on careers too amp x b this is my experience and i am not bashing the university i just want to tell my story i graduated with an associate s degree in computer science from a local community college i attended wgu briefly under the computer science program i excelled greatly in the cs program and was on track to graduate the program rather quickly however after months in the program i realized it was not for me the various cs and it courses are more focused on preparing for various certifications instead of developing highly proficient software engineers the courses were not rigorous or advanced for the bachelor s level many of the courses were the same material i learned at the community college level intro to artificial intelligence is a water down course that is provided to students without experience or education in linear algebra mind you linear algebra is a very important aspect of artificial intelligence wgu highest mathematical course for the major is calculus web development fundamentals focus on html and css at a very novice level data management and the other courses are all the same introductory material i completed at the community college level after talking to several recruiters and others in the industry it has been made apparent to me that having a computer science degree is not enough certifications are worthless in the software engineering world projects and networks are extremely important wgu does not have any resources or connections for its cs sd and other tech graduates to access my wgu mentor was rude and was not very helpful after my experience with wgu i have completed my computer science degree at the city university of new york and i am now employed as a software engineer at a well known start up making k a year junior year i successfully completed cuny tech prep a web dev or data science full stack program for cuny students to learn in demand technologies and network with industry partners i completed the program with one web development project by myself and i collaborated with another student in their data science project this helped put my python knowledge to work and build my skills cuny has tech pipeline programs into tech companies consulting firms and banking firms i attended career events and networking opportunities and built robust network connections senior year i completed a co op at a firm i completed a summer software development training in swift ios i completed single page applications multi page application and ar project that showcased what was taught since i had taken java and advanced data structures courses developing an andriod project was a breeze i was often contributing to open source projects after all is said and done it did take me and a half years to finish my bachelor s at cuny versus completing the degree at wgu at year and a half however cuny better prepared me for the industry educationally and professionally i graduated with a github full of many projects connections and no debt but the ones i chose to showcase were mobile applications ar application web dev application data science project that worked alongside a collaborator in the tech industry a degree as a box to check is not enough you have to build things beyond the novice level granted wgu expects you to seek out your own opportunities and resources beyond their curriculum but they should better prepare students for the ever changing tech world and create opportunities for their students to become proficiently prepared and help them meet future employers and collaborators they should pair them with mentors that work well with them although i did seek out my own opportunities the additional support from the cuny system truly bolsters my prospects exponentially opportunities i do not think i would of gotten if i stayed at wgu thank you to all the wgu students downcasting my posts because they don t like an alternative input']

Name: 144_cs_engineering_cs degree_systems
Representative_Docs: ['i m a freshman in cs so maybe not a ton more experience than you but i ll try to give some insight that i wish i had known senior year i would say one thing to be aware of that software engineering is different from a cs major most of what you learn in a cs degree curriculum will not directly apply to a typical software engineering career path keep an open mind and try to explore some other majors too like computer engineering information technology operations research etc the cs entry level field is pretty concentrated right now it s competitive there are cs project teams at my college with two technical interview rounds and single digit acceptance rates i ve applied to close to internships and have yet to find a summer internship so yes cs has some great salaries one of the upperclassmen i know got like a k sign on bonus at facebook for a full time role but you have to be proactive about it just getting a cs degree is no guarantee i also know a lot of students who came in with goals of becoming artificial intelligence machine learning robotics whatever hot field software engineers just be aware the more specialized and cutting edge roles will usually require a graduate degree with all that said cs is pretty fun and so is college in general even with covid way better than high school i ve met a ton of cool people and one of them even started a start up that i m involved in right now best of luck', 'i m currently about to pursue a ms cs at a well known cs school in the us they don t have any requirements as to what cs subfield i have to choose to specialize in i can choose to specialize in any of theory systems ai ml pl etc or i could choose not to specialize at all and take courses from a wide array of different subfields for breadth given that i m wondering which area of cs might be the best to go for i m interested in really most areas of cs and am especially leaning towards systems programming and theoretical cs a bit too but i am open to most subareas including ai ml however from a job market perspective i m wondering which subarea might make the most sense for example i ve heard that you need at least a master s in ml to do interesting work in ml data science whereas i haven t really heard that a master s in computer systems would lead to more interesting jobs so maybe that means that getting a ms specialized in systems would be useless since most jobs that require such systems backgrounds can be done with just a bs would a ms in cs with a concentration in systems or theory be wasting my ticket so to speak and should i try to specialize in ai ml as that would open more doors up in the future or are there any other specializations to look for that would be beneficial to enter the workforce for context i m mostly open to most areas in tech related to software whether that be swe ai ml researcher cybersecurity researcher etc i m still trying to figure out which path is the best for me and deciding my ms specialization is part of that thanks in advance', 'the market is a bit saturated but still better than other fields i have experience with the best way to make yourself marketable seems to be to specialize while still in undergrad for example you could focus on machine learning or graphics or simulating physics that can make you a much stronger candidate for some of the more specific jobs that not everyone is qualified for as far as pay goes it varies a ton on one end of the spectrum i ve heard of some software engineers at small companies making around k a year on the other end i believe google pays new grads a starting salary of around k a year more common subfields like web dev tend to pay less highly technical fields like data science and machine learning pay more the absolute most important thing you can do in college is to get as many internships as you can one every summer ideally those connections are what will really help you get a job later on and the work will help you to gauge whether you actually like the field or not even in cs finding a job can be really hard if you don t have internship connections beforehand one more thing if you are also getting a business degree you might want to look into the data science stuff knowing how to use ml algorithms to manipulate and analyze complex data can pair well with business savvy and could make you a very strong candidate for entry level data science positions it s a difficult field but it pays very well senior data scientists can make upwards of k a year although you would typically need a master s degree at that level edit swe internship pay is usually somewhere in the hr to hr range also i forgot to directly answer your question but i would definitely say a cs major is worth it at least it s as worth it as any college degree can be there still aren t a lot of programs with better job prospects than cs even if jobs aren t as plentiful as they used to be']

Name: 147_gpa_research_letter_transcript
Representative_Docs: ['i m a rising sophomore at uc berkeley but i m worried about my gpa i originally came into the school as an intended physics major with no coding experience but decided to try out cs during my first semester i loved what i learned in cs but because i literally didn t get how pointers actually work until months in i bombed my cs and ee classes and got a b b b in them also pledging a frat didn t help at all i decided to step up my performance the next semester and i managed to get an a a a in all my cs ee and science coursework this now puts me at a gpa which i m glad about the upward trend but at the same time worried about since it might not help me pass the resume screening at fang internships especially google because they ask for your gpa i know this sub emphasizes how gpas don t matter too much and that experiences matter much more but after seeing my friends who secured internships last year at apple amazon google etc have gpas of at least it made me a bit worried about my gpa i do have two ai ml related projects very basic only uses decision trees and alpha beta pruning a lot of front end experience and some back end experience if that helps', 'i m currently doing my undergrad right now at a canadian university uwaterloo that s kind of well known in silicon valley industry but not in academia i m concerned that my gpa after graduating will be too low to get into any prestigious masters phd program for something as competitive as deep learning currently i m at a gpa and if i put full effort into my coursework i can expect to be at a gpa by the time i graduate august i m primarily concerned about whether i need a graduate degree from a top us school in order to get research positions at big name tech companies like google microsoft etc whether my gpa allows me to reasonably expect to get into a us school that satisfies criteria i currently hypothesize that my low gpa is due to a lack of interest in the topics covered in university because i m really good at and have put a lot of research and effort into full stack engineering systems design and other practical skills that i don t learn in school but end up using during internships so i m pretty sure that if i set aside a few months to study i can get a near perfect score on gre or subject tests i m also fairly confident that if i work on networking i could get involved in a few deep learning research projects with profs at my school and some referrals it s mainly a gpa concern at this point because my next best alternative is likely to take a software engineering career right out of undergraduate', 'here s what a professor in ml at mit with nearly k citations has to say about gpa gt i think the gpa statistic itself doesn t matter much if a student seems interesting based on letters then i glance at their transcript a few bs or one c are not concerning something spottier than that that still improves to almost all a s is probably okay too but strong letters are a critical thing and can outweigh a lot of weak grades it can also potentially help to put a sentence in your statement or have your letter writers put a sentence into their letter saying something like as you can see from their transcript ziz had some trouble in their first two years but then clearly hit their stride and has been getting top grades and doing great research since then or was overcommitted or had some health issues or have now found their niche in systems or whatever gt if there s a single b c anywhere on a transcript it won t matter for grad school but with even one more b c in something like algorithms or machine learning and we would start at mit to be concerned still the concern could be tempered by a really strong research letter or be less strong at other very worthy universities gt i would say that the important thing to do is figure out what s leading to you not having the performance in this class that you re hoping for either it will be something that can change your health or level of business or study skills or too many other commitments or something that is less likely to your natural taste in kinds of work aptitudes etc that will help you decide whether classes like this but harder which is what you d need to engage with later in your career seem do able and even actually fun gt getting bs on your ml related classes is definitely a downer but the key thing grad schools are looking for is research the letter s from your research supervisors try to make it great not good and find universities that have some of the top published ml people but are not the big name schools for your phd what matters is who your supervisor is not whether your university is in the top read journals to see who is doing solid work in your area that isn t at a top place overall i ve heard that the top few places are swamped with applications while the next few are not and there are a lot of great researchers out there in ml right now the student they were responding to had the same gpa you have right now in brief graduate school is about research conduct good undergraduate research perform well in algorithms and ml and reap the rewards cheers']

Representative_Docs: ['if you want to go the cs route as a self taught programmer beware anyone with a degree will have an advantage and a lot of times a cs curriculum focuses on a lot of theoretical standard masters and usually come cheaper too if you want to improve your situation though more than just pursuing endless education i would advise that you actually just look for jobs in those fields or adjacent to those fields when you get there and poke your nose around then you can figure out what other training you would want to get yes it will be hard to get these jobs without experience but who cares apply to a lot of them find a place that is willing to take a bet on you and show them why you re worth taking a bet lastly i think another key career mistake people often make is thinking Name: 154_interview_projects_coding interview_portfolio
Representative_Docs: ['hey my knowledge of what gpa means is lacking because it s a us thing and i m in the uk however i ve hired plenty of people for graduate roles who haven t gotten top of the class grades but have performed excellently in interviews having some projects on github is a nice idea to show ahead of time that you can code and that you re interested perhaps apply some tutorials to some mini projects and put your code up there for example you might do a tensorflow tutorial and build a neural network that does something fun it doesn t even have to work it s just tinkering and learning practing for interviews maybe it doesn t hurt there s books like cracking the coding interview which you ll either find empowering or terrifying depending on your love of interview questions it varies from employer to employer how much they do questions like these i don t like them too much i d rather do something collaboratively', 'if its fang you are after you need to do more leetcode you do need projects to populate your resume and to have something to talk about but you should spend the bulk of your time on leetcode regardless of what you study i recommend spending at least hour every day on data structures algorithms now here s the thing with fang especially for junior roles its not software engineers who select your resume to interview you but its the hr recruiters by the time your resume comes to an engineer your interviews have already been finalized and we are just asked to interview you additionally no engineer i know has actually looked through a candidate s projects on github especially if you are a junior because we dont have time due to the number of candidates and because more likely than not the projects are not worth looking at you will learn how to write good code at the company as a reference when i graduated college i had projects a spring app deployed on aws a nodejs app not deployed anywhere an implementation of a low level secure data transfer protocol a mobile game i made using unity and a variant of a recurrent neural network that i had programmed from scratch they were not very complex but sounded nice on paper', 'getting your first software dev job comes at varying levels of difficulty in tiers of importance work experience gt coop gt personal projects gt grades you ve got a personal project great shows that you ve done something that s not a school bootcamp project it shows you re not just another person who went to school because they learned software dev pays well i wouldn t recommend putting this as an industry project because it s implied you ve tried to make money off of it but it s a great interview piece to describe to the recruiter how your program works to wow them tech stack doesn t matter as much as people think it does what matters more is that you as a candidate can problem solve because it s expected that you re going to be training for a while before contributing a meaningful amount it s much more important that you can demonstrate that you can break down a problem and provide a solution in a programming language of some kind anecdotally my current job is for c my coding interview was in java i had no experience in c and it was years since i last touched java the interviewers two sr developers liked that i was able to verbalize my thought process and understand the flaws in my code and how i could improve it for the questions what where the changes they faced through the journey you re going job to job and learning everything behind software development in an industry not a whole lot really changes on the corporate side lots of meetings and emails sometimes a company wants to experiment with a new framework other times it s just maintaining an existing system what areas did they have to grow in communication is the biggest aspect of actual software development the haha programmer is in the cave working is as big of a misnomer as ai is just if statements in a startup it can be pretty informal where you do a daily standup and code for the rest of the day but bigger companies you re going to be talking to sales management and other developers communication is basically one of the most important skills that a developer needs what is the gap like speaking from myself getting grilled by two sr developers at an interview pretty huge my coding interview was me demonstrating what i knew i finished their coding interview question in half the time that was allotted so they took the time to poke at everything i was inexperienced at and teach me extra things at that interview imposter syndrome is something that you ll feel around these people mind you these people worked in the industry for years they learned every trick in the trade and are still learning do you think i can do anything now to work on bridging that gap not really after you ve picked up your basic coding skills you really need to be working in the industry to know what it s really like industry software development is a whole new monster compared to what we learned at school bootcamp how much does a jr have to know to meet the expectations of the opportunity provider what are those expectations as a jr you are expected to have your hand held for a month or two sitting next to a more senior developer and pair programming with them sometime you are both working on the same thing and writing the same lines of code you are expected to ask questions and learn don t worry so much about meeting expectations you ve already met them at the interview']

down random rabbit holes', 'there are really four main ways we interact with language reading writing speaking and listening oftentimes schools overemphasize reading and writing especially in k and it s possible your sister needs some more focus on speaking and listening to strengthen those skills they usually develop first and weakness in reading and writing is best supported with systematic teaching of language rules like letter and sound patterns and remediating speaking and listening to help your sister learn how to manipulate sounds this means she could change the beginning sounds cat gt bat medial sounds tin gt ten and final sounds cat gt cap i am of the opinion the skills get rushed and are easy to practice at home even with older kids amp x b however sight word practice is most effective if you have strong phonemic and phonological awareness otherwise you re practicing something she might not fully understand yet i strongly suggest incorporating lots of rhyming books i know sometimes this can feel silly to older kids so as an older sibling maybe you can request more rhyming books and help show your enthusiasm and interest in them so you re modelling that excitement about reading for her as you read draw her attention to letter patterns in the book you could say i noticed that every time i hear the ay sound i see the letters ai you can ask her to find other words with that pattern or find her own pattern it doesn t have to happen often but start incorporating that when you read together maybe once or twice a book amp x b if she s still struggling with sight words more complicated skills like comprehension response interpretation and recoding putting together the letter sound patterns in a word might still be difficult for her it helps to remind her each time you read letters have names and sounds sometimes one letter has one sound sometimes we can combine two or three or even four letters to make other sounds whenever i read i like to look for the letter patterns in the words i would also try to increase the amount of time you read together read more books to her and have her read more books to you amp x b music is a great way to practice listen third graders still love to hear stories told aloud look into podcasts spotify has story read aloud playlists youtube channels or using things like bluegrass classical music or jazz to challenge her to
